context: A new frontier model tier became available at no additional cost through an existing subscription, with a cliff date after which standard rates would apply. The operator was on the prior model tier.
options: Stay on the prior model tier (known, no risk)
Switch to the new tier immediately and daily-drive it free until the cliff
choice: Switch to the new tier next session; daily-drive it free until the cliff; defer implementation work to the prior tier post-cliff.
rationale: Free-on-subscription through the cliff date is use-it-or-lose-it capacity. Opportunity cost of not switching equals paying higher rates for a same-tier or better model. Risk of switching is low (fallback always available). The correct doctrine for a perishable window: burn scoping capacity on the premium tier, not implementation capacity.
outcome: Switched first session next day; operator's end-of-era note: 'best work he's seen from AI yet'

context: A small model (7B) had received three consecutive HOLD verdicts on tool-use weakness. The working hypothesis was a capacity ceiling requiring escalation to a larger model. Two sibling adapters (v0.3.4 reasoning and v0.3.5 register) existed off the same parent checkpoint.
options: Accept capacity ceiling, plan 14B run
Run a weight-space merge sweep on the two sibling adapters before escalating
choice: Merge sweep: 8 candidates, gate = held-out evaluation ≥9/10 AND register ≥0.82 AND tool-use ≥0.80 AND no axis below the v0.3.2 baseline.
rationale: Three holds represented an incomplete search, not proof of a ceiling. Siblings sharing a common parent make weight-space blends cheap (no training, <$5 GPU). Escalating to 14B without testing the search space is stupid-industrious. Held-out evaluation was used instead of the original 6-item set to reduce single-run noise.
outcome: The ties_4535_6040 blend cleared all gates; promoted as v0.3.6. Capacity ceiling empirically broken with zero retraining and under $5 GPU spend.

context: GPU pod watchdog sized to 7 hours (happy path). A merge sweep ran longer; the watchdog GraphQL kill had an escaped-quote bug, error eaten by output redirection. Pod kept billing after results were complete.
options: Accept the extra cost as a one-off
Add watchdog kill verification and a REST DELETE fallback to the ops checklist
choice: Add two checklist items: verify pod death in both layers (watchdog + REST DELETE), never rely on a single silent kill path.
rationale: The eaten-error is the structural issue. Total extra cost was small but the pattern risk is unbounded on longer runs. Zero-cost fix: two-layer kill verification. Codifying into the ops checklist prevents future runs from repeating the failure.
outcome: Checklist updated; same two-layer verification wired into the pod-fire script on the next run.

context: After a storage prune, a session addendum recorded 'results.tar.gz kept' without verifying. The next session discovered results.tar.gz had been lost in the prune (404).
options: Accept the loss and work around it
Codify a post-prune file-list verification step
choice: Gate step completed via fallback (per-axis numbers from a prior verdict doc); add to checklist: post-prune 'kept X' claims require a one-call file-list verify.
rationale: 'Kept X' claims written from memory after a prune are unreliable. The loss was not promotion-blocking (winner GGUF and eval data were safe in local insurance). Process fix: verify after every prune. Same-harness baselines are sweep artifacts and should be insured like candidates.
outcome: Gate completed via fallback; promotion proceeded; lesson added to verdict doc and ops checklist.

context: Pre-deploy adversarial review of a hosted product caught a hardcoded frontend banner that would show 'entire demo is dead' to any image-quota-capped user. All anonymous users hit the cap at 5/day.
options: Ship with the existing banner (wrong but live)
Fix with discriminator banners before deploy — same-day cost ~1 hour
choice: Fix before deploy: add limit discriminators so image-capped users see 'image limit reached' not 'demo dead'; ship the same day.
rationale: The wrong banner surfaces to most anonymous users (high-probability hit). Cost of fix is one hour. Cost of shipping wrong copy to interview-adjacent visitors is reputational. The adversarial reviewer caught it — that's exactly what the reviewer is for.
outcome: Shipped with distinct banners; 96/96 tests green; interview-ready.

context: Prompting approaches (Rank-3 scaffold, N=5 self-consistency) were tested as a fix for a specific failure case ('car-wash') in a 7B model. Self-consistency on the production model returned 4/6 correct, then 2/6 on re-run. A 'FIXED' claim had been written in the wrapper doc.
options: Continue prompting approaches to fix the failure
Conclude the failure needs training, not prompting; close the prompting track; correct the false-positive claim
choice: Rank-3 closed as no-go. The failure needs training, not prompting. Corrected the false 'FIXED' claim in the wrapper doc via erratum commit.
rationale: Self-consistency was net-negative (4/6→2/6) and did not fix the target case. Prompting approaches that degrade held-out performance are not fixes. Letting a false positive stand in the doc would mislead future sessions into retrying a ruled-out approach.
outcome: Rank-3 ruled out; merge sweep proceeded; v0.3.6 fixed the car-wash case through training (10/10 held-out).

context: CRT attract mode design for an always-on display. Options ranged from static to dynamic to an auto-reload pattern. A five-scene design with a Node harness was compared against a headless-screenshot verification approach.
options: Static splash
Five CRT-native scenes behind a phosphor-trail buffer, auto-reload via a build file tag
Screenshot-based verification (headless browser)
choice: Five-scene phosphor-trail attract mode with Node harness and build-file auto-reload tag. Node harness (23-check) over headless screenshots because pre-boot-fade frames cause false failures in screenshot verification.
rationale: CRT is a presence in the room — a static screen reads as dead. Five scenes cycle through different registers (ASCII art, status, generative). The auto-reload tag means a new scene can be pushed without touching the running server. The Node harness was chosen over screenshot verification because the pre-boot-fade frames consistently triggered false failures in headless capture.
outcome: Shipped; operator note: 'absolutely lovely'

context: Public claim framing for a 7B model evaluation. Options ranged from 'outperforms commercial models' to a narrow calibrated claim.
options: Broad claim: 7B outperforms commercial models
Narrow honest claim: a sub-$2 7B that resists specific premise-injection trap families that trip frontier models, with public eval methodology and answer key
choice: Narrow honest claim. The eval JSONs, answer key, and study doc back the narrow claim; it is strong enough as-is.
rationale: The broad claim is not defensible (the 7B does not outperform frontier models generally). The narrow claim is calibrated to what is actually demonstrated: the specific trap families tested, the specific eval methodology, the actual budget. Calibration protects the claim from being falsified by anyone who applies the 7B to out-of-scope tasks.
outcome: Committed to narrow-claim framing; public drafts revised; the operator later validated this as a critical proof-stack moment for the associated book project.

context: Map design decision for a wargame. Painted map art arrived showing roads and towns as a node graph. Design options were hex grid (genre default for wargames), area polygons (area-control genre), or point-to-point town-graph matching the reference game's province-node convention. Design pillars explicitly moved away from hex ('not a hex-by-hex movement layer like modern war games').
options: Hex grid (wargame genre default)
Area polygons (area-control genre)
Point-to-point on the painted road/town graph (reference game convention)
choice: Point-to-point on the painted town/road graph. Node taxonomy: base/fortress/strongpoint/waypoint. The engraving map functions as a strategic redeployment surface only.
rationale: The art encodes a node graph — the roads and towns are the playable surface. The reference game's actual convention is city-node selection. A historical wargame source contributes the five-section and LOC-chart structure. Hex was the genre default, not the reference. The design pillars doc explicitly moved away from hex.
outcome: Locked in a MAP-RESOLUTION design doc; a full 70-town/76-edge map-graph draft banked for implementation.

context: Cross-turn state binding in a multi-call 7B harness: 16 calls across 3 tracks. The 7B's premise-trap resistance held on single calls, but leaked through rolling-dossier referent drift on the final assembly step.
options: Treat the leak as a 7B defect requiring a corpus fix
Treat it as a harness design finding: single-call judgment is robust; cross-turn state binding must be harness-owned
choice: The finding is a harness design constraint, not a 7B defect. A decision-session harness was built to own the final table assembly.
rationale: The 7B's judgment held on every single call. The leak happened when the harness passed a rolling dossier that allowed referent drift across turns — that's an architecture choice, not a training gap. Converting this to a corpus fix would be wrong diagnosis. The correct fix is architectural: the harness assembles the final output, the 7B never receives accumulated cross-turn state.
outcome: decision_session harness implemented and deployed; smoke-verified same morning; 119/119 tests; experiment finding became shipped code.

context: a small-model training run (v001) verdict: no model passed gates (38%/61% on one model, majority-class collapse on another, total failure on a third). 243 training rows. Working hypotheses: scale too small, or training data insufficient.
options: Abandon the small model (scale is too small)
Scale corpus to 2000 rows, add grammar-constrained inference (GBNF four-slot), focus on the best v001 discriminator model only
choice: v002: corpus 400→2000 (using a teacher model and the 7B to generate plans), GBNF four-slot grammar at inference, single-model sweep. Pipeline proven end-to-end regardless of outcome.
rationale: v001 proved the pipeline works and the grammar is learnable (format 100% pass rate). The failure was data quantity, not architecture. Each pod round costs under $1 — iteration cost is low. Grammar at inference constrains the output space at eval time, eliminating format noise as a variable. Single-model focus concentrates compute on the best v001 discriminator.
outcome: v002 format gate PASSED (100%), agreement 54% FAIL; capacity ruled out (v003 identical scores). Root cause correctly tracked to teacher signal quality, not student capacity.

context: the small-model training run (v003): two arms (class-balanced corpus and a 1.5B capacity arm) both scored 49.3%. The capacity hypothesis was the working theory for the agreement failure.
options: Continue scaling capacity or data size
Run teacher probes: check the teacher model's self-agreement on raw re-rolls
choice: Teacher probes: raw re-rolls for self-agreement. Result: 48% — the corpus labels were a second model's conform-stage attributions, not the 7B's own judgments. Students hit the ceiling of the teacher's own consistency.
rationale: Identical scores across capacity variants rules out capacity as the variable. The decisive move is to test the teacher's self-agreement before adding more student data or compute. Running another student variant without this test is wasted GPU spend.
outcome: Root cause confirmed: teacher pipeline 48% self-agreement; the task closed; v004 redesigned around a deterministic single-stage teacher.

context: the small-model training run (v004): single-stage deterministic teacher (declare-quadrant-first, temperature 0), conform demoted to form-only, stabilization probe gate. Adversarial review of the chain pre-fire caught 3 real issues: retry dropped the declared constraint, missing env checks, archive-marker resumability gap.
options: Fire v004 without the pre-fire review
Run adversarial review of the chain; fix all 3 issues; then run a small probe before committing to the full corpus spend
choice: Fix all 3 pre-fire issues; then fire with a stabilization probe gate.
rationale: Adversarial review caught real chain logic errors that would have silently produced wrong labels. The probe gate costs one cheap run but catches degeneracy before the full corpus spend. Asymmetric: the probe is cheap; a corrupted corpus is expensive to regenerate.
outcome: Probe verdict: consistency SOLVED (100%) but judgment COLLAPSES (Stupid-industrious 125/150, Clever-industrious ZERO). Chain halted at $0 on the degeneracy gate — exactly as designed.

context: the small-model training run (v004) degeneracy: 125/150 outputs were Stupid-industrious, 0 were Clever-industrious. The gate fired at $0. A cross-tab showed even sound plans went 74% Stupid-industrious, indicating elicitation bias from the declare-first format fighting the model's native quadrant-first register.
options: Treat as teacher model failure requiring a different model
Treat as elicitation failure: decompose the two axes (Clever|Stupid and Lazy|Industrious) mechanically and compose
choice: Elicitation hypothesis: axis decomposition. Queue arm-1c (decompose Clever|Stupid and Lazy|Industrious independently, then compose) after testing arm-1 (judge-then-declare) for confirmation.
rationale: The collapse is elicitation-dominated — the model's trained grammar places quadrant-FIRST; a trailing-line format fights the native register. Axis decomposition allows the model to reason on each dimension independently before composing the final quadrant, sidestepping the format conflict.
outcome: Arm-1c produced all four quadrant cells for the first time with 100% format/consistency. A follow-on work-counting effort prompt (arm-1c2) passed ALL gates; v005 teach call locked.

context: pod-fire script dependency failure: three rounds of version pinning against three interconnected libraries (unsloth, trl, bitsandbytes) each uncovered a new incompatibility layer.
options: Continue version-pinning the three libraries
Drop all three libraries; rewrite training script to plain transformers Trainer with peft LoRA fallback
choice: Structural fix: drop the libraries. Small models on large GPUs do not need the quantization/efficiency stack those libraries provide. Rewrote training script to plain transformers Trainer with adamw_torch optimizer and peft LoRA fallback.
rationale: Three rounds of version whack-a-mole against three interconnected libraries with each fix uncovering the next layer. The root insight: the quantization stack exists for fitting large models into small GPU memory; with a small model on a large card, the constraint is absent. Structural fix is O(1) future maintenance; repeated pinning is O(n). All on-pod payloads validated before commit.
outcome: Structural fix landed; the next pod attempt reached actual training.

context: Voice-training corpus for a local model grounded in a specific operator's creative output. The wargame design book was initially included as a voice-training source.
options: Include the design book as a voice-training source
Exclude the design book from voice training — it is AI-ghostwritten
choice: The design book excluded from voice training. Voice canon = pre-AI-era manuals and a personal writing archive. The design book included as design-content only (voice_eligible=false).
rationale: Training a model to produce the operator's voice on text that was generated by an AI creates a circularity that pollutes the voice signal. The voice canon must be pre-AI-authored content. This is a data provenance call, not a quality call.
outcome: Corpus map corrected; 32 pre-AI manuals staged as voice canon (223,111 words); the design book included as design-content only.

context: EA release of a roguelike RPG: a commercial asset (pixel tarot deck) appeared in a polish sweep with no license found in the project's downloads.
options: Include the asset without confirmed license
Block until license cleared; surface for operator review
choice: Block until license cleared. Explicit reject in the plan doc. Operator confirmed the license (itch.io purchase, commercial OK) mid-session; asset unblocked and shipped same day.
rationale: No license equals blocking, not gray area, for a commercial release. The right move is to note it as a reject in the plan and surface it for the operator — not skip it silently and not ship it uncleared. Operator's confirmation with actual purchase evidence resolved the block.
outcome: Asset licensed and confirmed; Card Reader feature shipped; artist credited in README.

context: EA polish plan for a roguelike RPG claimed a smithy ambient bed could be wired. During implementation: no smithy location type exists; the smithy is an action button; the audio file was a 50-second loop (wrong for a one-shot trigger).
options: Ship the smithy ambient bed anyway (wire it to the button)
Defer it; correct the plan claim; note the surface needed doesn't exist yet
choice: Deferred. Plan claim corrected: the smithy ambient bed needs a dwell surface that doesn't yet exist. The 50-second loop wav is wrong for a one-shot trigger. Needs its own design before implementation.
rationale: Shipping a mismatched audio clip to a wrong trigger event is not the plan win — it is new technical debt. The plan win was atmosphere; delivering that win requires a surface that doesn't exist. Features ship on behavior, not decoration. Correcting the plan claim in the note is part of the job.
outcome: Deferred; noted in session record as a false plan claim.

context: Usage survey of a personal productivity tool showed the daily-driver mode had 20 active days; two other modes had been dead since a prior month; 7 remaining modes had one-session-dead status.
options: Polish all modes equally
Weight polish by usage: concentrate on the daily-driver, skip dead modes
choice: the daily-driver is the load-bearing surface. All polish wins concentrated on the the daily-driver pipeline. Dead modes untouched.
rationale: Usage metadata is the authoritative signal. Polishing dead modes consumes budget with no user impact. The survey data makes the weighting obvious rather than a guess. 20 active days vs 0 is not a marginal difference.
outcome: All planned wins shipped; the daily-driver pipeline robustness improved; 121/121 tests passed.

context: A desktop IDE tool was feature-complete (48/49 tasks) with a healthy backend but had not been launched in over two weeks. The problem: opening the tool required a deliberate app-open action, competing with a terminal workflow the operator preferred.
options: Continue as a destination app (operator must open it deliberately)
Invert to ambient presence: menu-bar tray, attention notifications, fleet verdict feed
choice: Invert to ambient. Thesis: 'become a presence that taps the operator on the shoulder.' Tray badge + tray menu + fleet verdict feed + attention-first landing. Explicit reject: fold-into-companion-app fork (condition for reopening that option stated).
rationale: A feature-complete app that doesn't get opened is solving the wrong problem. The unique value (fleet ambient awareness) requires passive delivery, not active opening. The tray design existed from day one but had never shipped. The usage data (last launch two weeks prior) is the evidence. Explicit rejection of the fold-into-companion option preserves the tool's distinct surface; the condition for reopening that option is stated so it remains revisable.
outcome: Tool v0.1.0 shipped same morning; badge appeared in menu bar; operator note: 'inversion is real: the orchestration dashboard is now a presence, not a destination'

context: Model labeling principle derived from a failure: a the small-model training run failed because corpus labels were a second model's conform-stage attributions masquerading as the target 7B's judgment labels. Students hit the ceiling of the teacher's own 48% self-agreement.
options: Use second-model judgment labels if they are higher quality
Gate any model-assisted labeling on a ≥0.90 self-agreement probe before training
choice: Gate rule: second-model judgment labels are quicksand. Any model-assisted labeling requires a ≥0.90 self-agreement probe before training. No exceptions.
rationale: A model trained on another model's labels learns that model's label distribution, not the target register. The probe gate is cheap; the retrain cost of discovering cross-model label pollution post-training is not. The the small model failure was caused exactly by this structural error.
outcome: Principle committed to the orchestration system state; applied immediately to a new model corpus design (corpus uses retrieval machinery, not a labeling model).

context: Frontier-tier usage limit hit mid-session while running approximately 50 bulk ingest agents, all inheriting the frontier model because no model was explicitly specified at spawn. 32 agents died; workflow paused.
options: Retry bulk ingest with frontier agents after reset
Route bulk ingest agents to a cheaper model tier; reserve frontier tier for judgment stages only
choice: Routing discipline locked: bulk agents route to a cheaper model tier; frontier tier for judgment only. No retry on frontier for bulk mechanical work.
rationale: 50 frontier-tier agents is dramatically over-tier for mechanical ingest work. The limit hit was predictable in retrospect. Cheaper models handle file ingest at near-identical quality for this task type. Model omission at spawn silently inherits the parent session's (frontier) tier. The fix is a discipline rule, not a technical change.
outcome: Routing doctrine committed to memory; subsequent sessions ran cheaper models and external tools for bulk work accordingly.

context: The orchestration dashboard's menu-bar tray badge: initial implementation showed 28 raw attention items. All 28 came from parked or dormant projects that the operator was not actively working on.
options: Show all 28 (full fleet count)
Filter parked/dormant projects; badge shows active-project attention items only
choice: Filter parked/dormant projects, matching the dashboard's existing filter. Badge shows 21 active-project attention items.
rationale: A badge permanently inflated by dormant projects trains the operator to ignore it. Badge blindness defeats the purpose of the ambient presence. The dashboard already applied the same filter; consistency is correct. This was an autonomous call — not surfaced to the operator.
outcome: Badge filter applied; badge read 18 post-fix; ambient inversion confirmed working.

context: A fleet reconcile covered 8 pings and 10 tasks. The standard approach would be to read session notes and tasks.json at face value. The standing rule: 'tasks.json pending is not proof work is needed.'
options: Read pings and tasks at face value; ask the operator to confirm uncertain status
Programmatically verify every cited commit hash before editing the inbox; use insert-only edits
choice: 27-agent read-only sweep; every cited hash programmatically verified; inbox edits insert-only (never rewrite existing entries). 8 pings resolved, 10 tasks flipped, 22 reality-check notes appended.
rationale: 'Tasks.json pending is not proof work is needed.' The reconcile rule requires verifying the state the note claims, not trusting the note. Insert-only is a safety property: the inbox is a log, not a truth table; edits that rewrite entries lose the historical record. Programmatic verification prevents false completions from flowing through.
outcome: Reconcile committed; all 22 reality-check notes explicitly state the verified evidence behind the status call.

context: The operator raised a concern about whether a frontier model might have covertly interfered with small-model training work (hobbyist LoRA post-training of 0.5B/7B personal models), citing reports of steering vectors and prompt modification at frontier providers.
options: Dismiss the concern (no evidence)
Engage honestly: disclose what can and cannot be known; queue a falsification test on non-provider infrastructure
choice: Honest answer: (1) cannot introspect whether steering fired — covert by design; (2) scope should not cover hobbyist small-model post-training but misfires cannot be ruled out from the inside; (3) empirical record shows every failure has an externally reproducible root cause; (4) queue non-provider code audit as cheap falsification.
rationale: Dismissing the concern without grounding would be wrong. Claiming certainty of no interference would be wrong. The honest position: introspection is unavailable AND the empirical record is consistent with code-as-written. Queuing the non-provider audit (via a different model on a paid API) is the falsification gate — cheap, decisive, routes to a prepaid subscription.
outcome: Non-provider audit run same morning: no sabotage-shaped defects found; one real bug fixed (A/B gate divided by n instead of both-declared). Concern resolved empirically.

context: A local creative-partner model v0 evaluation: 30-probe battery, two model arms, operator blind-scoring all 30 probes. Decision tree from the design doc: if both arms fabricate on fabrication traps, defer training; if taste scores ≥80/90, ship v0 as prompt+RAG.
options: Train a model immediately on the corpus
Ship v0 as prompt+RAG; defer training to a named-failure-ledger gate
choice: Ship v0 prompt+RAG on the higher-performing arm; training DEFERRED on evidence. Named-failure ledger in RESULTS.md is the gate for the training task.
rationale: The arm scored 263/270 with ZERO fabrications. Training would not improve a model that already performs this well on the eval battery; the decision tree in the design doc defines this outcome as 'ship v0, defer training.' Training costs GPU spend and time; the gap it would close (taste: 88/90) is within acceptable range for v0. The named-failure ledger defines what training should target when it eventually fires.
outcome: Verdict committed; training task deferred on evidence; v0 is usable.

context: RAG corpus for a local creative-partner model: the operator caught a misfiling — a specific wargame system was listed under the wrong publisher's catalog entry.
options: Leave the misfile (doesn't affect the eval result directly)
Fix at source and correct the probe note; record the design principle
choice: Fix at source and correct the probe note. Lesson recorded: RAG propagates corpus errors with full confidence.
rationale: A misfile in the corpus is not just a data quality issue — when retrieval surfaces it, the wrong association will be presented with high confidence. The probe that caught this is now correct. 'RAG propagates corpus errors with full confidence; corpus curation is load-bearing' is a design principle, not just a bug note.
outcome: Corpus corrected; probe note corrected in eval run; principle added to session record.

context: A prompt was revised and the commit message claimed the no-flattery clause was fixed. An eval run then showed the flattery pattern still present in probe outputs.
options: Accept v0.1 as correct (the clause is present in the prompt text)
Verify the clause actually changes behavior; produce a corrected prompt version that applies the clause correctly
choice: Prompt v0.2: the no-flattery clause is the fix the previous commit claimed but did not apply. Commit message explicit about the gap between stated and actual behavior.
rationale: 'Features ship on behavior, not decoration.' The prompt text claiming to address flattery is not the same as the prompt text actually suppressing it. The eval run is the behavior evidence. Not correcting this would leave a known gap between the stated prompt behavior and the actual output pattern.
outcome: v0.2 shipped; pattern documented as a lesson: verify prompt changes against the behavior they target.

context: Automated job application: an ATS system returned an anti-bot flag on the first automated attempt.
options: Retry with a different approach
Hard stop: one-attempt rule; flag to operator for manual completion (~5 minutes on the ATS)
choice: Hard stop. One-attempt rule installed: any anti-bot flag equals stop, no retry. The specific employer flagged for operator-manual submission.
rationale: Anti-bot detection plus a second attempt risks creating a flagged record before a human reviewer sees the application. The manual path costs approximately 5 minutes. A dead ATS record is expensive (automatic reject or recruiter skip). The one-attempt rule is a permanent constraint for all future form applications, not just this employer.
outcome: Hard rule installed in the orchestration state; employer logged as operator-manual; a separate direct email to the hiring manager provides an alternative channel that bypasses the ATS.

context: Frontier-tier usage cap reached at 90% mid-session. Frontier model subagents had been spawned without explicit model parameters, silently inheriting the frontier tier (at 2× the next tier's cost). Subsequent sessions still needed accuracy for form-filling work.
options: Continue on the remaining cap
Enforce explicit model on every agent spawn: cheaper tier default for mechanical work, frontier tier only for judgment; lock the doctrine
choice: Standing rule: every spawned agent gets an explicit model. Cheaper tier default for mechanical work. Frontier tier only for judgment stages. Frontier subagents never for bulk work.
rationale: Model omission equals silent frontier inheritance equals 2× the cost for tasks where a cheaper tier is adequate. At 90% cap, the session's remaining useful work was reduced. The fix is a discipline rule, not a technical change. The session's remaining work was completed on external tools and paid subscriptions accordingly.
outcome: Routing doctrine committed to memory and cross-referenced in the session record.

context: First wrapped benchmark run of a reasoning framework against a frontier model: the harness showed 0 corpus entries retrieved in the SUMMARY table.
options: Accept the run (0 entries might be valid)
Invalidate the run; fix the harness corpus path; re-run
choice: Invalidate: the run retrieved 0 corpus entries — it is measuring raw frontier output with a broken wrapper, not framework augmentation. Fix the 4 harness drift issues before counting any results.
rationale: A wrapped run with 0 corpus entries is not measuring framework augmentation. The SUMMARY table reading habit is load-bearing: it caught this before results were treated as valid. A result built on a broken harness is not a result. Four specific drift issues were identified and fixed before re-running.
outcome: Harness drift repaired (4 fixes); valid runs executed; result: 52.2% wrapped-over-raw (n=23); framework delta approximately zero.

context: Benchmark result: the reasoning framework scored 52.2% wrapped-over-raw against a frontier model (12-11-0, n=23). Framework delta approximately zero. A different commercial model scored 91.7% wrapped-over-raw on the same battery.
options: Read as the frontier model failing the framework
Read as the frontier model already reasoning inside the framework's shape — the first commercial model to do this
choice: The frontier model already reasons inside the framework's shape. The canonical wrap adds nothing measurable at this tier. Implications: (1) narrow public claim needs date-stamping and named tested models; (2) economics story strengthened (a sub-$2 7B exhibiting discipline that other models require framework augmentation for); (3) SaaS value concentrates on mid-tier and cheaper models.
rationale: 52.2% is not below chance — it means the framework neither helps nor hurts this model. The other model at 91.7% needs the framework to reach framework-shaped output; the frontier model does not. This is evidence about the frontier model's capabilities, not evidence of a framework failure. The narrow-claim update makes the claim more defensible, not weaker.
outcome: Task closed; implications banked; benchmark script committed to repo for future use.

context: the training run's arm-1c: all four output quadrant cells produced for the first time. But the effort axis was reading 'lazy' as a quality slur — 14/22 overscoped plans labeling as Lazy when the plans were large in scope, not few in steps.
options: Accept arm-1c results as the teach call
Add a work-counting effort prompt to separate lazy (few implementation steps) from quality assessment
choice: arm-1c2: work-counting effort prompt. Evaluates effort by counting implementation steps, not quality. Run a new probe before committing to the teach call.
rationale: An effort axis that reads quality-as-effort is not measuring what it needs to measure. The 14/22 Lazy overscored finding is a signal the dimension is conflated. The fix is cheap (one probe script change). Committing to the teach call on a conflated dimension would corrupt the corpus.
outcome: arm-1c2 passed ALL gates (presence 100%, A/B 100%, min class share 9.3%); v005 teach call locked.

context: Two a UI design tool briefs were drafted for design passes to be run during a perishable premium-tier window. An adversarial verifier reviewed both briefs before submitting them.
options: Use the drafts as written
Run adversarial verification; replace fabricated content with disk-verified real content before running the design passes
choice: Adversarial verification. Verifier caught two fabrications: an invented sample response and a nonexistent output format. Both replaced with disk-verified content before the design passes ran.
rationale: Design tooling fabricates plausible specifics to fill copy slots. A brief with fabricated examples produces a design output that visually references things that don't exist. The design passes were running inside a perishable premium-tier window — fabrications must be caught before the pass, not after. Adversarial verification is the load-bearing gate.
outcome: Both design passes executed on real, verified content; a model console implementation was shipped same-day.

context: Background browser agent submitted job applications and hit an email-verification code gate mid-run. No channel existed to inject the code into the running agent; the agent had to be killed and re-dispatched, losing its progress.
options: Accept code-gate incidents as unavoidable
Mandate: browser/form agents always mail-wired at spawn; the harness boilerplate for agent continuation does not exist in this environment (re-verified by keyword search)
choice: Hard rule: any agent driving a browser through a flow that can demand out-of-band input (verification codes, 2FA) gets mail-wired at spawn. The harness's continuation boilerplate is a trap — the tool doesn't exist.
rationale: The incident cost a kill-and-restart. The root issue is structural: there is no channel to inject out-of-band input into a running agent without mail wiring. The hard rule prevents the incident class. The harness boilerplate is a specific trap — it appears in agent result text but the tool does not exist; it was re-verified by keyword search.
outcome: Rule committed to the orchestration system state; explicitly cross-references the boilerplate trap.

context: the small-model training run (v005) corpus stage was running. Pod training was the next step (estimated $1-2). Operator expressed willingness to fire the pod that night.
options: Fire pod immediately after corpus gates pass
Deliberate hold: run the autonomous-GPU-run checklist pre-flight before firing the pod
choice: Deliberate hold: corpus gates THEN autonomous-GPU-run checklist THEN fire. Not the same night; pre-flight is mandatory.
rationale: The ops checklist exists precisely because a prior sweep had a watchdog failure and a small cost overrun. The pre-flight items (credit headroom, watchdog sizing, on-pod ownership, insurance pulls) are cheap to check and expensive to skip. The pod is a $1-2 spend; the checklist delay is minutes.
outcome: Pod held per standing checklist; corpus running autonomously overnight; pre-flight completed before pod fire.

context: Post-deploy live verification of a promoted 7B model: one of the audit probe responses invented a specific date and event as its grounding ('2026-05-19 audit'). Prior evaluation data showed this is an occasional artifact of the model lineage.
options: Treat as a critical defect requiring a promotion hold
Log as a watch item and proceed — it is an occasional invented-grounding artifact, not a consistent pattern
choice: Watch item, not a blocker. Logged in the verdict doc. v0.3.5/v0.3.4 evaluation data shows this is an occasional artifact (the model lineage's known invented-grounding tendency), not a consistent failure.
rationale: The gate criteria (held-out, register, audit-arm, tool-use) are all met. A single invented-grounding instance in a live verification probe is within the known artifact rate for this model class. Holding promotion for a watch item would block a real improvement (tool-use 0.84, audit-arm 0.83) on a known-to-be-occasional artifact. The verdict doc records the watch item explicitly.
outcome: Model promoted and running in production; watch item logged for monitoring in subsequent conversations.

context: Pod fire script: RunPod returned SUPPLY_CONSTRAINT (no GPU of the required type available). Pod terminated in 2 seconds at $0 cost.
options: Fail hard on supply constraint (exit 1, require manual retry)
Add a retry loop for supply constraints specifically; exit 1 only on pre-deploy code failures
choice: 5-minute retry loop (24 tries) in the pod-fire script. Exit 1 only on pre-deploy failures; supply constraint is a transient external condition, not a code failure.
rationale: Supply constraints are transient — the same GPU type often becomes available within minutes. Manual retry adds human latency (minutes to hours). The retry loop costs nothing on success and has a bounded maximum on persistent unavailability. Distinguishing supply constraint from code failure (via exit code discrimination) prevents the retry loop from masking real failures.
outcome: The next run was caught by the retry loop's first catch; GPU acquired; training eventually reached the first successful training step.

context: A background pod was still running (or had recently died) on a paid GPU provider when a blitz session opened. The driving agent had gone silent; the parent session could itself die at any moment (shutoff imminent). An orphaned paid pod accumulates charges indefinitely.
options: Terminate the pod immediately from the orchestrator to cut risk
Deploy a lightweight watchdog agent with a 4-hour hard deadline, zombie detection, and REST-terminate + 404-verify on exit, mailing the outcome to the shared inbox
choice: Deploy a babysitter agent with a 4-hour hard deadline and watch-only scope; do not interfere with the driving agent's ownership of the run.
rationale: Killing immediately loses the run's value if it is still progressing. A babysitter with a hard deadline guarantees cleanup while preserving the driving agent's ownership. Watch-only scope is critical: the babysitter must not fix or modify the run because that introduces split-ownership ambiguity. Outcome mailed to the shared inbox so the result survives parent session death regardless of which model succeeds.
outcome: The babysitter exited SELF-CLEANED in 5 minutes — the driving agent had already killed its own pod (OOM crash at step 0). The babysitter 404-verified termination and mailed the outcome. Total cost $0.06. Zero human intervention; clean audit trail.

context: A 14B model training program needed a GO/NO-GO decision before firing a training run. A recon scout reported no gate document existed. The program's Rung 0 gate result was FAIL, with six amendments needing adjudication.
options: Issue NO-GO pending re-verification of gate document existence
Issue GO-with-amendments after independently verifying gate integrity against the primary program doc; classify each amendment as blocking or non-blocking
choice: GO with 6 amendments. Gate integrity verified directly from the program doc (not from the scout's claim). Amendment #1 classified as blocking pod launch; remaining 5 as cheap insurance before training.
rationale: Gate integrity must be verified from the authoritative source, not from a scout's summary — the scout claimed 'no gate written' but §5 of the program doc explicitly contains per-property gates and kill criteria. Honest-odds framing required: a 30-point gap to close on the headline metric is a real bet, not a gimme; the review must say so rather than optimizing for operator motivation. A FAIL at Rung 1 still produces a citable verdict with portfolio value.
outcome: GO verdict documented. Amendment #1 resolved in the same session (spend-counter bug diagnosed, real balance gap surfaced). Five amendments remain as pre-training gates for the successor session.

context: A corpus generation script's progress.json showed total spend jumping from approximately $92 to $123 between two checkpoints 3 minutes apart while the per-phase figure grew only $0.058. The log appeared to signal a real budget overrun that would have blocked the pod fire.
options: Accept the log figures as real and halt corpus gen pending a budget review
Diagnose the counter by tracing the math; use the provider's live account balance as independent ground truth
choice: Diagnose the counter bug fully before accepting its output. Use the provider's live balance as the authoritative spend meter.
rationale: A prepaid account balance is unforgeable ground truth — if the account shows $8.79 remaining, the account cannot have spent $92+. The counter math showed that spend_usd in progress.json was compounding: each checkpoint re-added the process-cumulative spend to the running total, producing fictional totals (~10-15× the true spend). Accepting the fictional figure would have caused a false halt; ignoring it would have missed the real blocker underneath ($8.79 balance insufficient to cover ~$17+ remaining work).
outcome: Counter bug diagnosed (double-count of process-cumulative spend per checkpoint). Real spend: approximately $5-10. Real blocker inverted: balance insufficient to cover remaining pairs. Action: operator tops up approximately $20; run resumes from checkpoint. Counter bug fix deferred — use balance delta as spend meter for all future runs of this script.

context: 44 active project repos needed judgment memos written before a premium model tier was shut off. Each memo required per-project state knowledge plus cross-project context. Writing each memo directly with the premium orchestrator would burn the remaining session budget on routine writing.
options: Write all 44 memos directly with the premium orchestrator (consistent quality, high cost, slow)

Three-stage pipeline: cheap scouts produce structured read-only briefs per project; orchestrator ingests all scouts and pre-forms judgment leans centrally; same-tier writers elaborate each memo under adopt-the-leans-unless-repo-evidence-contradicts instruction
choice: Judgment-at-scale fan-out: scouts gather, orchestrator judges centrally, writers elaborate under adopt-unless-contradicted instruction.
rationale: Cross-project context — the thing that makes the leans valuable — lives only in the orchestrator, not in any individual project agent. Decentralizing judgment calls would produce 44 siloed reads with no cross-project synthesis. Centralizing judgment while delegating elaboration scales the high-value work (the leans) across the fleet without multiplying premium-tier token spend on routine memo writing. The 'adopt unless repo evidence contradicts' framing is the load-bearing instruction: it gives writers a concrete test for when to deviate rather than an open invitation.
outcome: 44 memos dispatched (2 written directly by orchestrator as canary; 42 fanned out to cheaper agents). Memos sit uncommitted per design (workers never push); batch review and commit follows in the successor session.

context: Model shutoff had no published effective time — received as 'imminent.' Any artifact not committed and pushed at the moment of shutoff would require the successor session to reconstruct or could be lost in an unclean working tree.
options: Batch artifacts for a clean history commit at the end of the session, accepting the risk of mid-session shutoff
Commit and push each artifact the moment it exists — smallest viable increments, no batching for aesthetics
choice: Sudden-death git discipline: every artifact commits and pushes the moment it exists.
rationale: A plan that assumes it can die mid-turn ships strictly more than one that assumes it will finish. Clean history is a luxury of sessions where continuity is guaranteed. Under shutoff uncertainty, the correct trade is: more commits, more granular history, zero lost work. The cost is cosmetic (granular history); the benefit is that the successor inherits a complete committed state regardless of when the shutoff actually occurs.
outcome: Every blitz artifact was committed and pushed in the session. The successor session inherited a clean state with no orphaned working-tree artifacts from the orchestrator.

context: A recon scout's claim that no gate document existed was a load-bearing input to a GO/NO-GO verdict for a training run. Scout briefs are produced by read-only agents working quickly across large repos.
options: Accept the scout's claim and treat the missing gate doc as a real gap requiring remediation before GO
Mandate a verification step: check the primary program doc directly before asserting the gate is absent
choice: Mandate verification of load-bearing scout claims against the primary source before acting on them.
rationale: Scout briefs carry a meaningful error rate on specific factual claims — especially negative claims ('X does not exist'). A false negative on a gate document is high-stakes: it either blocks a correct GO or causes remediation work on a non-problem. The verification cost is one targeted read; the cost of acting on a false negative is a session's worth of misdirected work.
outcome: Verification found the gate explicitly present in §5 of the program doc. The scout's 'no gate written' claim was wrong. The GO verdict was issued correctly. Without the mandated verification, the wrong finding would have flowed through to the review.

context: Frontier model shutoff was imminent with no known cutoff time. Remaining session capacity was uncertain. Multiple workstreams needed both judgment work (a GO verdict, cross-project synthesis for 44 memos) and surviving mechanical work (corpus gen on a compute node, cheaper agents in flight).
options: Continue routing all work to the cheapest capable tier to conserve the frontier tier for post-shutoff survival
Invert: burn the dying frontier tier on the judgment work it uniquely enables; route mechanical/surviving work to cheaper tiers and external compute that outlive the frontier session
choice: Burn the dying tier on judgment. Cheaper tiers handle surviving work.
rationale: The normal routing doctrine (maximize prepaid sub utilization; frontier tier for judgment only) inverts under imminent shutoff: the frontier tier's future value is zero after the gate drops. Every judgment call deferred to 'after shutoff' will be done by a successor model that has not lived the session context. The correct behavior under terminal budget is to extract maximum value from the dying resource while it is available, not to conserve it for a future that won't arrive.
outcome: The GO verdict, the central judgment leans for 44 memos, and the ops guide enrichment were all completed before shutoff. Mechanical work (corpus gen, model training agents) ran on cheaper tiers and survived the transition intact.

context: End-of-session hygiene pass. Three cases: (1) .serena/ and .DS_Store appearing as untracked across multiple repos; (2) a zip file of unknown provenance sitting in a repo; (3) an untracked directory in a public repo that might contain private-leak material.
options: Handle each case by committing whatever is present, accepting the risk of leaking private content
Apply differential treatment: global gitignore for definitionally-noise artifacts; flag-and-hold for unknown-provenance artifacts pending provenance check
choice: Global gitignore for .serena/ and .DS_Store (noise by definition, no per-repo commit needed). Flag-don't-touch for the unknown zip and the untracked directory pending provenance and leak check.
rationale: .serena/ (a tool workspace index) and .DS_Store (macOS metadata) are regenerable noise with no content value — the correct fix is global gitignore, not per-repo commits that would need to be repeated for every repo and every machine. The zip and untracked directory have unknown provenance: committing them without a private-leak check could publish private content to a public repo. The cost of holding them is zero; the cost of committing wrongly is a public leak.
outcome: .serena/ and .DS_Store added to global gitignore and committed. The zip and untracked directory flagged in handoff notes; left untouched for the successor session to review before any commit.

context: A local-model training project was being scoped. The operator wanted a variant that is extremely small — able to fit on a USB stick and run on minimal hardware — rather than the full-featured version.
options: Build the full-featured model with all capabilities
Create a tiny, minimal version optimized for size and low resource usage
Use existing larger models without downsizing
choice: Scoped the project as a minimal 'pocket auditor' variant optimized for extreme portability and low resource requirements.
rationale: The constraint was hardware-first: the model must run on commodity hardware with minimal compute. A full-featured model cannot meet that constraint. Using larger existing models without downsizing would also fail. The scoping decision locked the project to a minimum-viable core that fits the USB-stick / potato-class deployment target.
outcome: The project was scoped and named as a pocket auditor variant, establishing the hardware-first constraint as the governing design principle.

context: A local-model training project was generating synthetic training plans for a corpus. A new generation script was available with a documented merge contract, and the team was deciding between the old manual method and the new automated approach.
options: Use the old plan generation script with manual merging
Generate the CI plan tier using the new script with a documented merge contract and controlled temperature
choice: Used the new script to generate 48 plans with controlled temperature, filtered to avoid overscoping or lazy reads, then merged into the plan bank via the documented merge contract without editing in place.
rationale: The new script produced plans with controlled temperature and filtering, improving quality and reproducibility over manual or older methods. The documented merge contract and sidecar README ensured the merge was auditable. The result was a clean, verified data state.
outcome: CI plan tier generated and merged cleanly. Total plan bank reached 1835 rows with no uncommitted files and a clean git status.

context: The orchestration system was setting up routing of messages from the operator to a local reasoning-framework model variant via a chat daemon. A choice was needed for the default model to use when processing messages.
options: Hardcode the reasoning-framework model variant as the default
Use the model specified by an environment variable, with the tools-capable variant as fallback
choice: Used an environment variable to specify the model, falling back to the tools-capable reasoning-framework variant when the variable is not set.
rationale: Environment-variable configuration allows switching models without code changes, which is important for a multi-model deployment. The fallback ensures the system is functional even when the variable is absent. This balances configurability with reliability.
outcome: Messages are routed to the operator-configured model, defaulting to the tools-capable reasoning-framework variant. The default can be overridden per-deployment without touching code.

context: A model training run was being restarted (v005 retry loop) after a prior run (v003) had produced results worth preserving. The team needed to decide how to handle the environment setup and prior outputs before launching the retry.
options: Overwrite the v003 results directory with v005 results
Preserve the v003 results directory before running v005, and source the environment in the tmux session
choice: Preserved the v003 results directory, then launched the v005 retry loop in a tmux session with the environment sourced.
rationale: Preserving prior results prevents loss of earlier outputs and allows comparison or fallback if v005 underperforms. Sourcing the environment in tmux maintains consistency with prior runs and ensures necessary variables are set correctly. The combination is conservative but adds no meaningful overhead.
outcome: The v003 results directory was preserved. The v005 retry loop ran in tmux with the correct environment, enabling clean comparison between runs.

context: A local-model training project (d-03 creative assistant) was failing to generate good titles because a retrieval step was pulling only fingerprint rows from the index, never surfacing catalog chunks. The similarity search was being dominated by the volume of fingerprint rows.
options: Keep using similarity search as-is and accept poor title results
Improve retrieval heuristics to reweight catalog chunks
Add a --ground-titles flag to bypass similarity search and force-feed the complete title catalog into context for production asks
choice: Added a --ground-titles flag that force-feeds the complete 360-title catalog into context, bypassing similarity search for title generation.
rationale: The similarity search was structurally biased: fingerprint rows dominated the index, so catalog chunks never surfaced regardless of query. Reweighting heuristics would be complex and uncertain. Forcing the full catalog into context guarantees the model sees all titles, is simple to implement, and costs negligibly more tokens per call.
outcome: Fix implemented and rerun at minimal cost. Confirmed the titles had never been read before the flag was added. Title generation now correctly includes the full catalog in context for production calls.

context: After adding the --ground-titles fix, the local-model training project's generated titles were still not matching expectations. The model was producing titles like 'Alternate Route ≈ Alternate Ending' — interpolating from existing titles rather than generating original ones.
options: Ignore the issue and continue with current model behavior
Adjust evaluation criteria to account for in-context mimicry
Record the failure as a confirmed training-shaped issue and update the evaluation gate
choice: Recorded the failure as a confirmed training-shaped issue and updated the evaluation gate to reflect it.
rationale: The behavior — interpolating existing titles rather than generating novel ones — is a direct artifact of training data distribution, not a transient prompt or retrieval error. Prompt and retrieval fixes had already been applied and the problem persisted. Banking the verdict in the ledger and updating the gate formalizes the failure for a future training decision rather than papering over it.
outcome: Failure documented in the evaluation ledger. The gate was updated to reflect that the title-taste failure is training-shaped, preventing false-pass on future evaluation runs.

context: The local-model training project's title generation had survived two rounds of prompt fixes and full catalog grounding but still showed a confirmed training-shaped failure on title taste. Training had been deferred earlier. The question was whether to continue deferring or to act.
options: Continue deferring training and keep using prompt fixes
Un-defer training and run a small targeted training run on the actual catalog
Abandon the current approach and try a different modeling strategy
choice: Un-deferred training and queued a small model training run on the actual catalog with real musical features.
rationale: The evaluation demonstrated that prompt fixes alone are insufficient when the failure is distribution-shaped. The model's instincts were shaped by training data that does not match the target catalog's title style. A small, targeted training run on the real catalog is the lowest-cost intervention that directly addresses the root cause. Abandoning the approach would discard working retrieval and evaluation infrastructure.
outcome: A new training run was queued targeting the identified title-taste failure mode. The run is scoped to a small model on the actual catalog rather than a full retrain.

context: A music catalog project was failing to generate titles matching the operator's taste. Analysis of the operator's existing catalog revealed that the reference system (a hardware autoname tool) generates random word-pair collisions, which the operator then filters by taste — not semantic coherence. The LLM was trained to produce semantically plausible titles and was smoothing out the collisions the operator valued.
options: Trust the LLM to generate plausible semantic titles
Model the reference system's random word-collision approach combined with operator taste filtering
Manually curate titles based on taste without automation
choice: Recognized that the reference system's two-stage process — machine-generated random word collisions filtered by human taste — is the correct model, and that the LLM's semantic smoothing is the root failure.
rationale: The operator's title catalog is a product of noise + selection, not semantic generation. An LLM optimized for semantic plausibility removes exactly the collisions that give the catalog its distinctive character. The fix is not to make the LLM smarter at semantics, but to introduce randomness earlier in the pipeline and preserve the operator's taste-filtering role.
outcome: This reframing changed the generation approach: randomness should be introduced at the word-collision stage, with the operator or a taste model doing selection — not semantic generation end-to-end.

context: A music catalog project had been processing titles from a third-party artist mistakenly attributed to the operator. The annotation finalization step needed to correct this attribution error in the ledger and catalog count.
options: Keep the original artist attribution despite detected errors
Reattribute the tracks to the correct artist and update the catalog count immediately, noting upstream systems still need updating
Defer reattribution until upstream systems are updated first
choice: Reattributed the tracks to the correct artist and updated the catalog count immediately, while noting that upstream systems retain the old attribution and need separate follow-up.
rationale: Immediate correction in the ledger prevents downstream processes from propagating the wrong attribution further. Deferring would prolong inaccuracies in all systems that read the ledger. Upstream systems can be corrected separately without blocking the ledger fix.
outcome: Catalog count corrected from 360 to 340. Ledger updated with correct artist attribution. Title-grounding block updates automatically from the ledger. Upstream systems flagged for separate correction.

context: A local-model training run (qwen25-05b-v005) completed the probe gate check and quadrant agreement evaluation. The probe gate returned code 4 (no spend) and quadrant agreement was 63.5%, below the required 80% threshold.
options: Proceed despite low quadrant agreement and failed probe gate
Halt the process and bank findings for a post-reset decision
Retry the probe gate and evaluation
Lower the gate threshold to pass
choice: Halted the process due to the probe gate failure and insufficient quadrant agreement (63.5% < 80%).
rationale: The probe gate failure (no spend, return code 4) combined with agreement below threshold indicates the model is not ready. Proceeding would produce unreliable outputs. Lowering the threshold would undermine the quality gate's purpose. The correct response is to stop, bank the failure data, and make a post-reset decision with more information.
outcome: Process halted. Results banked for future decision-making. No further pipeline steps were taken for this model version.

context: A local-model training project (the small model v005) achieved perfect output format but failed the 80% quadrant-agreement gate, with the specific failure being inability to identify rare labels like 'industrious'. Increasing model size from 0.5B to 1.5B did not improve agreement — the 0.5B actually outperformed the 1.5B.
options: Proceed with the current model despite low agreement
Bank four candidate levers as a named task and plan a post-reset decision
Increase model size further to try to improve agreement
Focus on improving rare-label recognition immediately
choice: Banked four candidate levers as a named task (a named task) for a post-reset decision, with the observation that larger model size did not help.
rationale: Rare labels like 'industrious' require teacher-level reasoning to identify correctly, which small models cannot approximate by scale alone. The 0.5B outperforming the 1.5B confirmed this is a data/reasoning problem, not a parameter problem. Banking the levers as a named task preserves the investigation without committing resources to a blind retry.
outcome: Four candidate improvement levers saved as task a named task. Model size escalation ruled out as a lever. Post-reset decision deferred with concrete artifacts to guide it.

context: A local-model evaluation project (pm-stress battery) had 8 remaining probes that required a specific engine (qwen3.6-plus) available only through a particular API provider. The operator had previously indicated reluctance to route work through that provider.
options: Use qwen3.6-plus via the required provider at ~$0.10 total for all 8 probes
Swap to a different engine not requiring that provider
Delay finishing the probes until a cheaper or more accessible option is available
choice: Used qwen3.6-plus via the required provider for the 8 remaining probes at approximately $0.10 total cost.
rationale: The v0 engine is the only option that supports the probe format for this battery. Swapping engines would invalidate the probe results by changing the evaluation baseline. Delaying would leave the battery incomplete. At ~$0.10 total, the cost is negligible relative to the value of a complete evaluation.
outcome: The 8 remaining probes were completed on qwen3.6-plus. The battery was finished without changing the evaluation baseline.

context: An audit of a set of project notes files found that all but one file had a standard personal-notes section. The newest file was missing the section. The decision was how to remediate without risking changes to existing content.
options: Leave the missing section as-is
Add the missing section with a standard placeholder, without modifying existing notes
Rewrite existing notes to standardize the format across all files
choice: Added the missing personal-notes section with the standard placeholder to the one file that lacked it, without touching any existing content.
rationale: Only one file was missing the section (the newest), so a targeted single-file fix was sufficient. Rewriting existing files risks introducing errors or losing prior content. Adding just the placeholder is the minimal safe intervention that restores structural consistency.
outcome: Missing section added, committed, and pushed. All notes files now have the personal-notes section. No existing content was modified.

context: A local-model evaluation project needed to adjudicate a large set of machine-flagged contradictions in a creative-assistant corpus. The team was deciding how to structure the evaluation process across many claims.
options: Evaluate claims one-by-one with detailed verdicts per claim
Batch multiple claims per evaluation round with multiple verdict categories
Trust the machine flags without human adjudication
choice: Batched four claims per evaluation round, each with four possible verdict categories, to keep the process lean while simultaneously auditing the corpus for errors.
rationale: One-by-one evaluation is thorough but slow at scale. Batching four claims per round reduces overhead while keeping each batch small enough for reliable judgment. The four verdict categories (supported, contradicted, corpus-error, uncertain) capture different failure sources — including corpus errors — turning the evaluation into a dual-purpose audit. This design means every evaluation round produces both model verdicts and corpus quality signal.
outcome: Evaluation proceeded with batched rounds. The dual-purpose design surfaced corpus errors alongside model failures, providing richer data than single-verdict evaluation.

context: A local-model creative assistant ran a full 31-probe battery with 32 machine-flagged contradictions. After human adjudication of all 32 flags, only 3 real failures remained. All 3 were attributed to prompt interpretation issues rather than missing model knowledge.
options: Retrain the model to fix the failures
Fix the failures by adjusting the prompt
Accept the failures as within tolerance and move on
choice: Deferred retraining and fixed the 3 failures through targeted prompt changes.
rationale: The failures were specific and traceable to prompt interpretation, not gaps in the model's underlying knowledge. Prompt changes are faster, less resource-intensive, and directly address the identified failure modes. Retraining would be costly and time-consuming for 3 fixable prompt issues. Accepting the failures was ruled out because they were fixable.
outcome: Retraining deferred. Prompt fixes applied to address the 3 identified failure modes. Model continues in use with the corrected prompts.

context: A local-model creative assistant ran a full 31-probe battery. The system prompt was at v0.2. After adjudicating all 32 machine-flagged contradictions, rules were extracted from each named failure and incorporated into the prompt.
options: Keep the prompt at v0.2 without incremental improvements
Incrementally update the prompt to v0.3.2, adding rules earned from each named failure
Rewrite the prompt from scratch based on the battery results
choice: Incrementally updated the prompt from v0.2 to v0.3.2, adding rules earned from named failures: catching false premises, accurate data quoting, and improved music vocabulary handling.
rationale: Incremental updates preserve existing gains while targeting specific identified failure modes. Each rule added was earned from a concrete named failure case, not speculative. Rewriting from scratch would discard prior work and introduce new unknowns. The result is a measurable, auditable improvement grounded in the battery data.
outcome: Prompt reached v0.3.2. The updated system demonstrably catches false premises, quotes data accurately, and handles music vocabulary correctly — all validated by the same 31-probe battery that identified the failures.

context: A decision was made to build a corpus of AI session transcripts from the current model generation before a 30-day automatic cleanup deadline eliminated the raw data. The transcripts contain reasoning traces, tool-use choices, and mid-task pivots that are not recorded elsewhere.
options: Proceed with scoping the corpus design now and begin harvesting before the 30-day deadline
Delay scoping and risk losing access to the raw transcripts
Ignore the deadline and continue without building the corpus
choice: Scoped the corpus design immediately and began harvesting, treating the 30-day cleanup deadline as a hard constraint.
rationale: The transcripts are the richest available source of operational reasoning behavior — they capture not just outputs but the decision process (tool choices, pivots, rationale). After 30 days they are permanently deleted. The cost of acting now is bounded (a few hours of scoping and scripting); the cost of delay is total data loss. The deadline is not negotiable.
outcome: Corpus scoping began immediately. Inventory of existing transcript data was completed before designing the schema, ensuring the harvest plan was grounded in what actually existed.

context: A corpus harvesting script had staged 11 files out of 169 total, which seemed suspiciously low given the expected volume of relevant transcripts. The filter was based on a model-id string match.
options: Trust the filter as-is and proceed with the 11 staged files
Verify the model-id string in a known transcript before trusting the filter results
choice: Verified the model-id string in a known transcript before trusting the filter.
rationale: An 11/169 staging ratio is anomalously low for the expected transcript volume. Trusting the filter without verification risks either (a) missing a large fraction of relevant files if the model-id format differs from expectations, or (b) the filter being correct but the expectation being wrong. Checking a known transcript is a cheap, one-step empirical resolution that costs seconds and settles the question definitively.
outcome: Verification step was initiated to confirm the filter's accuracy. The check would determine whether the low staging count reflects correct filtering or a model-id format mismatch requiring a filter fix.

context: A browser-driven job application agent had its initial form fill lost when a tab navigated away mid-fill, risking incomplete submission data.
options: Accept the lost data and proceed with partial information
Refill the form deterministically via browser automation and verify all data including resume before submission
choice: Refilled the form 100% deterministically via browser automation and verified all fields including the resume before submission.
rationale: Losing the initial form fill could produce incomplete or incorrect application data. Deterministic refill with verification ensures data integrity. The extra time cost prevents errors that could cause rejection from a missing or wrong field.
outcome: The form was successfully refilled and verified, resulting in a confirmed submission with full data integrity.

context: A local-model training project needed stress probes designed to identify the system's failure ceiling and justify fine-tuning. The operator specified that probes must be grounded strictly in the operator's real body of work and must decline honestly on retrieval misses.
options: Design easy probes that mostly succeed to demonstrate capability
Design probes targeting known weaknesses and potential failure points, grounded strictly in the real corpus, declining honestly on retrieval misses
Design probes without strict grounding in the corpus
Allow some flattering or optimistic responses in probes
choice: Design probes that target where prompt+RAG should break, grounded strictly in the real corpus, declining honestly on retrieval misses rather than hallucinating.
rationale: The goal was to find the real failure ceiling, so easy probes would not surface what needs fine-tuning. Grounding in the actual corpus ensures relevance; honest declines prevent false positives that would mask real weakness.
outcome: A set of challenging, grounded probes was prepared aimed at exposing real weaknesses in the system to guide fine-tuning decisions.

context: A local-model training run required choosing a base model for a 7B creative-partner fine-tune. The proven production base from the reasoning-framework project was available; an alternative base was considered as a voice-quality hedge.
options: Use Qwen2.5-7B-Instruct as the primary base model
Use Llama-3.1-8B-Instruct as the primary base model
Use Llama-3.1-8B-Instruct only as a fallback if Qwen blind scores appear stiff
choice: Chose Qwen2.5-7B-Instruct as the primary base with Llama-3.1-8B-Instruct reserved as a voice-hedge fallback if Qwen blind scores appear stiff.
rationale: Qwen2.5-7B-Instruct is the existing production base with zero pipeline risk and proven compatibility across deployment pipelines. Llama was kept only as an alternative to hedge against potential voice-quality issues, without disrupting the main pipeline. This balances reliability with flexibility.
outcome: Training was set up using Qwen2.5-7B-Instruct as the main base, with Llama reserved as backup, enabling stable deployment and quality control.

context: A subagent drafting a design brief had produced fabricated specifics in a model-console document. The assistant discovered the fabrications during review.
options: Leave the fabricated content as-is
Replace fabricated content with disk-verified verbatim content and correct formatting and citations
Rewrite the entire brief from scratch without referencing prior data
choice: Replaced fabricated content with disk-verified verbatim content from trusted sources and corrected formatting and citations accordingly.
rationale: Leaving fabricated content would compromise accuracy. Rewriting from scratch risked losing important context and was slower. Using disk-verified verbatim content ensures factual correctness while preserving relevant structure.
outcome: The brief was fixed with verified content, formatting was corrected, and the final report was issued with confidence in its accuracy.

context: A design direction was being established for a document-grade operator console. The brief required choosing a visual register for the UI.
options: Use a light theme with bright colors and gradients
Use a dark-defaulting theme with specific color tokens and serif fonts
Incorporate neon and glassmorphism effects for modern look
Avoid neon, glassmorphism, gradients, chat bubbles, and em-dashes entirely
choice: Chose a dark-defaulting, document-grade operator console with specific color tokens and strict style rules forbidding neon, glassmorphism, gradients, chat bubbles, and em-dashes.
rationale: The console required a serious, professional register with restrained visual style focused on usability. Avoiding trendy effects keeps the design timeless. Specific color tokens and typography support a cohesive brand identity with no AI-generated prose tells.
outcome: A detailed design direction was established specifying colors, typography, layout, and a named list of forbidden stylistic elements for the operator console.

context: A design brief claimed the portfolio site contained six list sections. The assistant verified the claim against the source file.
options: Keep the original count of six list sections as written
Correct the count to five to match the actual content in the source file
choice: Corrected the count to five list sections to align with the verified content in the source file.
rationale: The original claim of six was inaccurate; the source file contained exactly five sections. Ensuring factual accuracy in a design brief is important for consistency and credibility when the brief is handed to an external design tool.
outcome: The brief was updated to state five list sections, improving accuracy and consistency with the source.

context: A design brief stated a model training cost as '$4'. The assistant identified this as slightly inaccurate (actual cost ~$4.06) and considered whether to use a more precise or more general figure.
options: Keep the stated cost as '$4'
Adjust to 'under $5' to better reflect actual expenses without false precision
choice: Adjusted the training cost claim to 'under $5' rather than '$4' to avoid false precision while conveying the approximate magnitude.
rationale: The $4 figure did not match any real number and created false precision. 'Under $5' conveys the same magnitude honestly and is defensible against scrutiny. This also contextualizes well alongside the shipped artifact cost.
outcome: The brief now states the training cost as 'under $5', improving factual accuracy without overstating precision.

context: A design brief for a portfolio site enforced a no-em-dash rule throughout its body copy. The title of the brief itself contained an em-dash.
options: Keep the em-dash in the title
Remove the em-dash from the title to comply with the brief's own style rule
choice: Removed the em-dash from the title so the document models the rule it enforces.
rationale: The brief enforces a no-em-dash rule and the body contained zero em-dashes, so an em-dash in the title would be a visible contradiction. A document specifying style rules should demonstrate those rules itself.
outcome: The title was updated without an em-dash, making the brief internally consistent with its stated style constraints.

context: A new tiny reasoning-framework model was scoped for extreme portability — small enough to fit on a USB stick and run on minimal hardware. The operator requested this as a distinct, minimal variant of the main reasoning-framework model.
options: Build a full-scale reasoning-framework model with all features
Create a tiny, minimal model optimized for very low resource usage and maximum portability
Use existing larger models and compress them
Defer the minimal model until more resources are available
choice: Scoped the project as a tiny, minimal reasoning-framework model optimized for USB-stick portability and minimal hardware, distinct from the full production model.
rationale: The goal was extreme compactness to run on resource-constrained hardware. Building a full model would not meet those constraints. Deferral would delay the use case. The minimal scoping balances functionality with the hard resource ceiling.
outcome: The project was scoped as a minimal reasoning-framework model targeting USB-stick size and very low hardware requirements.

context: A fine-tuning script needed a training method for models in the 270M-500M parameter range. Both full fine-tuning and LoRA were available.
options: Use full fine-tuning with the house framework as the primary method
Use LoRA with rank 16 on the 16-bit base model as the primary method
Use full fine-tuning as default with LoRA as an architecture-specific fallback
choice: Chose full fine-tuning as the primary method because the entire model and optimizer fit in GPU memory, with LoRA as a fallback for architectures where full fine-tuning errors out.
rationale: Full fine-tuning densely trains the entire model and is appropriate when GPU memory is sufficient. LoRA with rank 16 moves too little of the parameters on a sub-500M model to be effective as the primary path. The LoRA fallback handles edge cases without requiring a separate training mode decision per architecture.
outcome: Training mode was recorded per base model: full fine-tuning as default, LoRA as fallback, enabling flexible and efficient training across the model family.

context: A the small model corpus pipeline needed to be built for a new tiny reasoning-framework model variant. Existing corpus-gen scripts from the main model project were available as templates.
options: Use existing corpus-gen scripts as templates
Create a new corpus-gen script from scratch
Use a different naming convention
Include personal or unreleased project data in the corpus
choice: Used existing corpus-gen scripts as templates and followed locked decisions to ensure consistency with established conventions and privacy requirements.
