choice: Implement majority rule with a tie-breaker to produce deterministic outcomes from the multi-card draw.
rationale: Majority rule with a tie-breaker ensures the mechanic always resolves to a clear outcome without leaving the event in an undefined state. A pure random draw is simpler but produces outcomes that feel arbitrary rather than symbolic. The majority rule also rewards players for understanding the suit distribution, adding a layer of engagement beyond raw luck. Integrating with the existing effect vocabulary avoids no-ops.
outcome: The tarot spread mechanic was implemented with majority rule and tie-breaker and integrated into the event system and UI. Verified and documented before commit.

context: A reference document for a tabletop RPG supplement (a regional travel map) had been committed with fabricated legend entries and a wrong index example.
options: Keep the fabricated legend and wrong index example as originally committed
Correct the fabricated legend and wrong index example against the actual map plates
Remove the legend and index example entirely
choice: Corrected the fabricated legend and wrong index example against the actual map plates.
rationale: Fabricated specifics in reference material mislead users who rely on the document for game preparation. The original commit introduced fiction into a factual reference, which erodes trust in the entire document. Removal would reduce usability; correction restores accuracy without losing structure. The cost of verifying against the map plates was bounded; the cost of propagating incorrect reference data is borne by every user who relies on it.
outcome: The rewritten subsection contains accurate legend and index information verified against the actual map plates.

context: A QA invocation was run on a roguelike RPG Godot 4 project, passing all gating tiers (import, asset lint, load/compile, and combat probes). The QA report identified several Tier 3 vision issues, including clipped bottom text on the options screen and overlapping map labels on the overworld status bar.
options: Ignore the Tier 3 vision issues for now and focus on higher priority bugs
Address the two issue-severity Tier 3 vision problems immediately as easy polish wins
Defer all Tier 3 vision fixes to a later release
choice: Address the two issue-severity Tier 3 vision problems immediately as easy polish wins.
rationale: All gating tiers passed, indicating stability. The Tier 3 issues are mostly advisory except for two flagged as issues impacting UI clarity. Addressing them now improves polish without risking stability; deferring misses an opportunity for quick quality improvements.
outcome: Plans were made to fix the bottom text clipping on the options screen and the overlapping status bar labels in the overworld HUD panel code.

context: An early-access polish plan was being synthesized for a roguelike RPG, and the debug mode toggle was identified as a low-effort, low-risk improvement: it exposed debug features to players in shipped builds.
options: Implement debug mode toggle without gating
Gate debug mode behind OS.is_debug_build() flag to prevent debug features in shipped builds
Defer debug mode changes
choice: Gate debug mode behind OS.is_debug_build() flag to prevent debug features in shipped builds and reduce risk.
rationale: Players should not access debug features in production — doing so could break gameplay or reveal internal state. The change required only three small code edits and was safe. Alternatives either risked exposing debug features or delayed a useful fix.
outcome: The debug mode toggle was gated behind the build flag with three small code edits and verified by manual release-export testing.

context: A local-model evaluation needed to run on a compute node where the ollama.exe process runs on Windows but the GGUF model files were stored on a WSL2 ext4 filesystem. The Windows process cannot read WSL2 ext4 paths directly.
options: Serve the models directly from WSL2 paths (not feasible due to Windows process limitations)
Copy the GGUF model files to a Windows filesystem staging directory and create Modelfiles there for ollama.exe
Use a network share or other cross-OS file access method
Run the evaluation entirely within WSL2 without involving Windows ollama.exe
choice: Copy the GGUF model files to a Windows filesystem staging directory and create Modelfiles there for ollama.exe.
rationale: The Windows ollama.exe cannot access WSL2 ext4 paths. Copying models to a Windows-accessible directory allows ollama.exe to load them. Creating bare Modelfiles with a FROM directive pointing to the Windows path preserves embedded chat templates and ensures compatibility. Other options were rejected due to complexity or integration constraints.
outcome: The models were copied to a Windows staging directory and Modelfiles created there, enabling ollama.exe to serve the models for the evaluation.

context: A batch process to create Modelfiles for multiple local models stopped after the first create operation, leaving two models (gemma/smollm2) unregistered.
options: Rerun the entire batch process
Rerun only the two failed create operations individually
Wait for a fix before rerunning
choice: Rerun only the two failed create operations individually to complete the update for the remaining models.
rationale: Rerunning only the failed operations avoids redundant work and quickly resolves the incomplete update without reprocessing the already-successful first model.
outcome: The two create operations were rerun individually, completing the model registration.

context: Three local model candidates were evaluated against agreement gates for a local-model evaluation run. All three candidates failed the evaluation gates. Among them, one model (Qwen) was identified as the strongest despite failing.
options: Use Qwen despite failure to salvage sample verdicts and proceed with cleanup
Use a weaker model
Retry or fix the gates
Abort the process
choice: Proceed with Qwen as the strongest option despite failure, collect sample verdicts, then perform cleanup and copy-back.
rationale: Although all options failed the evaluation gates, Qwen was the strongest among them, making it the best candidate to salvage useful output from. This approach balances the need to move forward with imperfect inputs rather than aborting or retrying immediately.
outcome: Sample verdicts were collected from Qwen, followed by cleanup and copying back results.

context: Three CRT shader packs were available for integration into a roguelike RPG (Godot 4 project). The options differed in origin and compatibility: one was a native Godot 4 project, the others likely required porting.
options: Use the native Godot 4 CRT shader pack (retro_crt_shader_pack_v1) requiring zero porting
Use one of the other two shader packs that might require porting or have less compatibility
Develop a custom shader pack from scratch
choice: Selected the native Godot 4 shader pack as the best fit because it requires no porting and is fully compatible with Godot 4.6+.
rationale: The selected pack is a native Godot 4 project containing multiple useful shader effects combined into a master shader, simplifying integration. The other packs likely require porting or are less compatible, increasing development effort. This choice minimizes work and maximizes compatibility.
outcome: The native shader pack was identified as the best of the three and recommended as a drop-in solution for the project.

context: An early-access roguelike RPG build was under review to identify gating factors before proceeding to the next development thrust. Two candidates were on the table: a voice pass on draft prose, and a live playthrough of a specific narrative branch.
options: Proceed immediately to the next development thrust without further verification
Complete the operator's in-play voice pass on draft prose first
Conduct a live headed playthrough of the narrative branch before moving on
choice: Complete the voice pass on draft prose as the next step, with the playthrough deferred.
rationale: The voice pass is a critical quality gate to ensure narrative and dialogue are polished before further development. The narrative branch had been probe-verified but never hand-played; the voice pass was prioritized to solidify current content. This balances polishing existing content with verifying gameplay experience.
outcome: The voice pass was scheduled as the immediate next step; the playthrough and subsequent decisions were deferred until after.

context: A personal productivity tool's scheduled check-in completed successfully, but the Telegram MCP server was unavailable and could not deliver the message.
options: Wait for Telegram MCP to become available and retry delivery
Route the message to a fallback local file as per the Step 4 fallback procedure
Skip message delivery entirely
choice: Routed the message to a fallback local file (state/checkins/2026-06-09-undelivered.md) per the documented Step 4 fallback.
rationale: Since Telegram MCP was unavailable, relying on it would delay or lose the message. Using the fallback ensured the message was safely stored for later delivery. Skipping delivery was not acceptable as the check-in data needed to be preserved.
outcome: The message was saved locally, committed, and pushed to the repository, ensuring no data loss despite the delivery failure.

context: A multi-hour GPU run for a local-model training sweep was being monitored on a rented pod. The on-pod watchdog timer was discovered to be set to terminate the run before the full evaluation and results-upload stages could complete.
options: Keep the watchdog timer at the original cap, risking premature termination before results upload
Extend the watchdog timer to allow the full evaluation and upload to complete
Disable the watchdog timer entirely to avoid interruption
choice: Extended the watchdog timer to a 10-hour cap to ensure the full evaluation suite and results upload could complete without interruption.
rationale: The original timer was too short and would have caused the pod to terminate before the final results upload, wasting the entire sweep. Extending balances safety with allowing the process to finish. Disabling the timer entirely risked runaway processes, so extension was the safer choice.
outcome: The watchdog was restarted with the new 10-hour cap, preventing premature termination and allowing the sweep to continue through all stages.

context: Three TIES merge-blend candidates were being evaluated for promotion against multiple gates: held-out reasoning, register overall, audit arm, and explanation arm scores. Each had different failure modes.
options: Promote ties_4535_5050 despite low register and audit arm scores
Promote ties_4535_6040 which cleared both the reasoning gate and the register gate
Promote ties_4535_d07 which had mixed audit arm results but a perfect explanation arm
choice: Promoted ties_4535_6040 as the first candidate to clear both the reasoning and register gates simultaneously.
rationale: The decision prioritized candidates meeting multiple stringent criteria simultaneously. ties_4535_d07 had a perfect explanation arm but its audit arm was below threshold. ties_4535_6040 uniquely passed all key gates, indicating superior overall quality. This aligned with predictions from the merge study.
outcome: ties_4535_6040 was identified as the front-runner and first candidate to clear both reasoning and register gates, marking a significant milestone in the training run.

context: During a model merge sweep, blending a prior model version (v0.3.2) back into the candidate set failed to recover audit register performance and caused a drop in held-out reasoning. The team had three SLERP blends with increasing pull toward v0.3.2 available to test.
options: Blend v0.3.2 back in at a single ratio to recover audit register performance
Test the three SLERPs with increasing pull toward v0.3.2 to find a better trade-off empirically
Abandon blending v0.3.2 due to poor audit arm results
choice: Proceeded with testing the three SLERPs with increasing pull toward v0.3.2 to see if any blend ratio improves audit without hurting reasoning.
rationale: The single-ratio blend failed on both reasoning and audit arm, making the hypothesis that blending back in would recover audit register weak. The SLERPs offer a dose-response curve to explore different blend ratios empirically. This allows evidence-based selection rather than abandoning the approach outright.
outcome: The team planned to evaluate the SLERPs next to determine if any blend ratio could improve audit metrics without degrading reasoning.

context: A held-out reasoning-trap evaluation revealed a specific failure mode: the model correctly identified two separate historical events in a question but avoided making a definitive impossible-premise rejection, instead giving a soft evasion.
options: Mark the failure as a soft evasion and accept partial correctness
Flag the failure as a failure to issue a clean impossible-premise call and treat it as a distinct failure mode
Ignore the failure and focus on overall score
choice: Flagged the failure as a soft evasion rather than a clean impossible-premise call, documenting it as a distinct failure mode requiring targeted improvement.
rationale: The model correctly parsed the premise components but failed to issue a definitive rejection, which is the load-bearing behavior for reasoning-trap robustness. Recognizing this as a named failure mode (soft evasion vs. clean impossible-premise call) directs future training more precisely than accepting partial credit.
outcome: The evaluation documented a specific failure taxonomy that informed development priorities for the next training iteration.

context: A pre-interview security audit of the reasoning-framework project's hosted product (with 25 agents) discovered that real subscriber personally identifiable information (PII) was committed to git history in a handoff document, even though the repository is private.
options: Ignore the PII exposure since the repository is private
Flag the PII exposure as a medium-severity issue and address it before the interview
Notify stakeholders about the GDPR/privacy leak risk without immediate action
Delay the interview until the issue is resolved
choice: The audit flagged the PII exposure as a medium-severity issue requiring remediation before the interview.
rationale: Although the repository is private, committed PII poses a privacy risk if the repository is ever made public or new contributors are added. Ignoring it risks GDPR violations and customer privacy breaches. Proactive remediation before the interview mitigates these risks and ensures compliance.
outcome: The audit completed with a clear finding guiding remediation to protect subscriber data before the interview.

context: A pre-interview audit of the reasoning-framework project's hosted SaaS site (run with 25 agents) discovered that nine links on the site incorrectly pointed to the private repository instead of the public one, and pricing information was outdated relative to the current tier split.
options: Leave the incorrect links and outdated pricing as is
Fix the nine links to point to the public repository and update pricing information
Remove the open-source and pricing claims until fully verified
choice: Fixed the nine incorrect links to point to the public repository and updated the pricing information to reflect the current tier split.
rationale: Leaving incorrect links would cause interviewers to encounter contradictory information, damaging credibility. Removing claims would reduce transparency. Fixing the links and updating pricing ensures accuracy and aligns public claims with reality.
outcome: The incorrect links were fixed, pricing was updated, changes were deployed and verified live, eliminating contradictions ahead of the interview.

context: During evaluation of eight blend recipes in a model merge sweep, the strongest SLERP blend (t07) showed a clear dose-response curve failure: t05 was the sweet spot and t07's register dropped sharply beyond it. A reasoning-score verification for t07 was still pending.
options: Include t07 in the final ranking and wait for reasoning score verification
Exclude t07 from the final ranking because the poor register score made the reasoning score irrelevant for ranking
Delay final ranking until all pending verifications complete
choice: Excluded t07 from the final ranking because the register failure made the reasoning score irrelevant for ranking.
rationale: The dose-response curve showed a clear pattern with t05 as optimal and t07 performing significantly worse. Even a strong reasoning score would not offset a register failure of this magnitude; verifying it would not change the ranking. Excluding t07 avoided wasting resources on a non-competitive blend.
outcome: The final candidate set was built without t07, and Stage B's full test suite was started on the remaining candidates.

context: A free-demo interface for the reasoning-framework project's hosted SaaS enforced a daily image generation limit. When only the image-turn quota was exhausted, the system was displaying a single 'demo exhausted' banner that also blocked text turns, prematurely ending user sessions.
options: Show a single combined usage limit banner for all turn types when either limit is hit
Show separate usage messages for photo turns and text turns to preserve text-only continuation
Show no usage limit banners to free users
choice: Showed separate usage messages for photo turns and text turns so users could continue text-only sessions after photo turns were exhausted.
rationale: A single 'demo exhausted' banner when only photo turns were used up was killing the demo funnel by preventing users from continuing with text turns. Separate messages inform users that text turns remain available, preserving engagement while still enforcing the image limit. The adversarial reviewer caught this before deployment.
outcome: The updated implementation correctly informs users when photo turns are exhausted but text turns remain, preventing premature funnel abandonment.

context: At the end of a model merge sweep evaluation, TIES 60/40 and SLERP middle blends were measured against three metric thresholds: held-out reasoning score, voice/register score, and audit arm score. Multiple other blends failed on one or more thresholds.
options: Select TIES 50/50 blend as finalist
Select TIES 60/40 and SLERP middle blends as finalists based on passing all three thresholds
Select a different blend or discard all finalists
choice: Selected TIES 60/40 and SLERP middle blends as finalists because they both met or exceeded thresholds across all three evaluation dimensions.
rationale: The selected blends scored highly across reasoning, voice, and audit arm, while other blends failed to meet voice or audit arm thresholds, or had lower reasoning scores. Meeting all three criteria simultaneously was the selection gate.
outcome: The two blends were locked in as finalists for Stage B evaluation.

context: After an unattended multi-hour GPU training run with a near-miss (watchdog timer and pod self-destruct issues), the team needed to decide how to preserve the operational lessons learned for future runs.
options: Keep the lessons only in personal memory
Write lessons in a temporary note
Create a canonical committed document (checklist) covering the failure modes, cross-referenced in personal memory
Rely on ad-hoc verbal reminders
choice: Made the lessons permanent in a canonical committed checklist, personal memory, and a third location for redundancy.
rationale: A committed checklist ensures future unattended GPU runs follow best practices. Personal memory supports automatic pre-flight checks in future sessions. Multi-pronged preservation balances permanence, accessibility, and automation to reduce risk of repeat failures.
outcome: The checklist was committed to the reasoning-framework project repo, establishing a permanent operational reference for future unattended GPU runs.

context: A new local-model version (Finalist #1) was evaluated against multiple performance gates including tool-use judgment, anti-engagement, interface-aware closing, self-state honesty, anti-meta-leakage, voice, and polemic symmetry. It passed all gates with strong scores and surpassed the current production model on tool-use judgment.
options: Promote the new model to production immediately given strong scores
Defer promotion pending baseline comparison tests, treating the model as a strong candidate
Reject the model despite strong scores
choice: Deferred promotion pending baseline comparison tests while treating the model as the leading promotion candidate.
rationale: The model demonstrated superior performance across all scored gates, including surpassing the production model's tool-use judgment score. However, baseline comparison tests were still pending, so immediate promotion was deferred to ensure comprehensive evaluation before deployment.
outcome: The model was cleared for promotion pending baseline tests, marking it as the strongest candidate to date.

context: A new model version (v0.3.2) was evaluated on a held-out trap set. Previous training iterations had a whack-a-mole problem: fixing reasoning caused regressions in voice or tool-use.
options: Fix reasoning but accept regression in voice quality
Fix voice but accept regression in tool-use
Achieve improvement across reasoning, voice, and tool-use simultaneously without regression
choice: The new version achieved improvement across reasoning, voice, and tool-use simultaneously without trading any dimension away.
rationale: Previous attempts to fix reasoning caused regressions in voice or tool-use. This version closed all three loops simultaneously, achieving a 10/10 on the held-out trap set while maintaining personality guardrails and tool usage. This resolved a persistent multi-dimensional whack-a-mole problem.
outcome: The new version scored 10/10 on the held-out trap set, maintained personality guardrails, and resolved the whack-a-mole problem for the first time.

context: Two finalists remained in a model merge sweep evaluation. Finalist #2 failed the tool-use gate by repeatedly retrying tools instead of surfacing blockers when stuck — a behavior inconsistent with the desired auditing posture. The 60/40 TIES blend had cleared all gates.
options: Promote Finalist #2 despite the tool-use gate failure, given its strong conscience scores
Promote the 60/40 TIES blend as the only candidate to clear every gate
Hold the promotion decision until further testing
choice: Promoted the 60/40 TIES blend as it was the only candidate to clear every evaluation gate.
rationale: Finalist #2's tool-use failure — retrying tools indefinitely rather than surfacing blockers — was a critical behavioral flaw that outweighed its strong conscience scores. The 60/40 TIES blend passed all gates (reasoning, register, audit voice, tool-use), making it the only unambiguous winner.
outcome: The 60/40 TIES blend was selected as the winner and candidate for production promotion.

context: After an unattended GPU run, the pod's self-destruct mechanism was supposed to trigger automatically on completion. An email alert about storage limits arrived, prompting a manual check.
options: Trust the pod self-destruct to have worked and not verify
Manually verify the pod status and kill it if it was still running
choice: Manually verified the pod was still running and killed it manually, since the self-destruct had silently failed.
rationale: Although the pod was supposed to self-destruct, it was found still running and accruing billing charges. Verifying and manually killing the pod ensured no further charges accrued. This prioritized cost control over blind trust in automation.
outcome: The pod was manually killed, stopping further billing and freeing storage.

context: A deployed RPG session-zero advisor was dogfooded after integrating it into a chat interface. During testing, the soft commit trigger was causing unintended campaign creation when users were still exploring options.
options: Keep the existing soft commit trigger that might cause accidental campaign creation during exploration
Add a hard-commit rule requiring explicit 'go ahead' phrases before creating campaigns
choice: Added a hard-commit rule so that only explicit 'go ahead' phrases create campaigns.
rationale: The soft commit trigger was causing unintended campaign creation, confusing users and creating errors during the exploration phase. Requiring a hard commit with explicit phrases ensures user intent is clear before a campaign is created, improving reliability at the cost of slightly more friction.
outcome: The hard-commit rule was implemented, deployed, and verified, preventing accidental campaign creation during dogfooding.

context: A newly registered RPG system (Hieronymus) uses literal Bosch paintings as its hex-map worldscapes — the paintings are the playable terrain, not decorative illustrations. The question was whether naming the paintings in the system digest was sufficient for an LLM GM to run the game.
options: Ship Hieronymus with just a named digest listing the seven paintings
Include rich, accurate painting references folded into the digest with detailed GM descriptions for each region
Use AI-generated images to replace the paintings
choice: Include rich, accurate painting references folded into the digest with detailed GM descriptions and public-domain scan sources.
rationale: Since the worldscapes are literally the paintings themselves and integral to gameplay, a digest that only names them would not provide enough context for the GM or players to navigate or describe regions. Incorporating detailed descriptions grounded in actual images and scholarship makes the game runnable. Using AI-generated images was rejected in favor of public-domain scans for accuracy.
outcome: Plans were made to gather public-domain scans of the seven paintings and author detailed GM descriptions for each region, enhancing the digest with rich visual and scholarly context.

context: A Quartz-based static site's Open Graph image plugin (og-image) was generating WebP images per page, but Facebook does not support WebP for OG image previews, causing OG image failures on Facebook.
options: Keep the og-image plugin enabled and try to modify it to generate Facebook-compatible images
Disable the og-image plugin and rely on the static default PNG fallback in Head.tsx
Replace the plugin with a different OG image generation mechanism
choice: Disabled the og-image plugin by setting enabled: false in quartz.config.yaml, allowing Head.tsx's static PNG fallback to activate universally.
rationale: The og-image plugin registers as CustomOgImagesEmitterName and suppresses the static fallback in Head.tsx, injecting per-page WebP images that Facebook does not support. Disabling the plugin activates the static PNG fallback that Facebook accepts. This is simpler and more reliable than modifying or replacing the plugin.
outcome: The fix was applied successfully, resulting in clean OG image generation compatible with Facebook.

context: Before deploying a new RPG system registration to a compute node running a local chat bot, the assumed vault path on the target machine was discovered not to exist. The bot was also unreachable from the WSL environment due to a networking boundary.
options: Proceed with the deploy despite not knowing the real vault path or bot reachability
Map the real deploy plumbing (vault clone location, bot's RPG_VAULT_DIR, restart token, bot reachability) before deploying
choice: Mapped the real deploy plumbing before firing the deploy to ensure correct deployment conditions.
rationale: Deploying without verifying the vault path and bot reachability could lead to deployment failures or inconsistent state. Mapping the plumbing first confirms all necessary components are correctly set up, reducing risk. Deploying blind would be guessing.
outcome: Deployment was paused to map the actual plumbing, preventing errors from deploying against incorrect environment assumptions.

context: A published Quartz 5 site's theme had garish peach-colored links and low-contrast text in dark mode. A contrast fix had been made locally that morning but was never committed or deployed — confirmed by inspecting commit history, which showed the recent commit did not touch color files.
options: Adjust CSS variables to fix contrast and readability to meet WCAG AA standards
Leave the peach color and low-contrast text as is
Wholesale replace the theme with a different one
choice: Adjusted dark mode CSS variables to change links from peach to goldenrod, achieving contrast ratios of 14× and 8.28:1, meeting WCAG AA.
rationale: The original dark mode CSS block was reasserting a problematic color value. Adjusting the CSS variables produced a clean, maintainable fix that preserved the warm parchment aesthetic while meeting accessibility standards. The change was minimal and reversible with a one-line swap.
outcome: The stylesheet update was deployed and verified live; all color values meet WCAG AA standards.

context: A latent bug was discovered in the CRT advisor path: it validated RPG system slugs against a frozen hardcoded list that was missing newer entries (e.g., knave-2e), causing valid commits to be silently rejected by the advisor.
options: Hand-add missing slugs to the frozen hardcoded list
Validate against the authoritative KNOWN_SYSTEMS set in rpg.mjs as a single source of truth
Ignore the issue and keep current validation
choice: Validate against the authoritative KNOWN_SYSTEMS in rpg.mjs to eliminate drift between the advisor's list and the full catalog.
rationale: Hand-adding slugs risks further drift and future silent rejections as new systems are added. Using the authoritative KNOWN_SYSTEMS ensures validation is always up to date and consistent with the full catalog without requiring manual maintenance of a frozen list.
outcome: Planned to confirm KNOWN_SYSTEMS is cleanly exportable and fix the latent validation bug, eliminating silent rejections of valid system commits.

context: After deploying new code to a compute node, the bot restart API call returned a 400 error while the health check returned 200 (bot still running old code). The 400 error's root cause was traced to CRLF line endings in the .env file on the Windows filesystem, which introduced a stray carriage return character into the token header.
options: Restart the bot again to reload files with the corrected token header
Pull files again before restarting
Ignore the 400 error and continue without fixing the header
choice: Restarted the bot after stripping the CR and quotes from the token header, without pulling files again.
rationale: The files were already correctly deployed on disk; the issue was the stray carriage return from Windows CRLF in the .env file corrupting the header value. A clean restart after fixing the header value was sufficient to reload the corrected files. Pulling files again was unnecessary overhead.
outcome: The bot restarted successfully with the corrected token header, resolving the 400 malformed header error.

context: After fixing a CRLF-corrupted token causing a 400 error on the bot restart endpoint, the new code was running. The question was whether to trust the fix or verify it end-to-end by calling the affected API endpoint.
options: Trust the restart and token fix without further checks
Perform a non-destructive behavior check by calling the /api/rpg/advisor endpoint to verify the fix end-to-end without creating any side effects
choice: Called the /api/rpg/advisor endpoint non-destructively to verify the fix end-to-end.
rationale: While the restart and file verification indicated the fix was applied, exercising the actual endpoint confirms the fix works in practice. A non-destructive call (reading advisor config, not creating a campaign) allows verification without side effects.
outcome: The endpoint returned the expected parsed config with the new RPG system accepted, confirming the fix worked end-to-end.

context: A proposal was made to give a locally-trained 7B reasoning model an 'emotional state.' The concept was ambiguous — it could mean a stylistic register that changes tone, a tracked queryable state variable, or a fully-felt emotional experience that could influence reasoning outputs.
options: Implement emotional state as a stylistic register or voice that changes tone but not judgment
Implement emotional state as a tracked, queryable state variable that influences reasoning
Implement emotional state as a fully-felt emotional experience that could distort reasoning and audit output
choice: Recommended the operator clarify which interpretation they mean before implementation, noting that the stylistic register is safest and fully felt emotions are highest-risk.
rationale: Each interpretation carries different safety implications for a reasoning-and-audit model. A stylistic register preserves reasoning integrity (low risk). A tracked state variable adds complexity but is manageable. Fully felt emotions risk distorting audit outputs — the model's primary value proposition. Clarifying intent upfront prevents building the wrong thing or introducing unintended behavioral drift.
outcome: The operator was advised to specify which emotional state interpretation they want before proceeding, enabling safer and clearer implementation scoping.

context: A local reasoning-framework model was exhibiting austerely functional output; the team wanted to add warmth without rebuilding. The model already scored high on the self-state axis, which was designed to prevent fabrication of self-state.
options: Rebuild the model to enable richer emotional expression
Lightly tune the system-prompt register to add brief warmth without rebuilding
Leave the current austere register unchanged
choice: Try lightly tuning the system-prompt register first to add warmth, before any rebuild.
rationale: Rebuilding risks reintroducing lying failures and engagement-optimization behavior the prior training work eliminated. The high self-state score indicated the model already had structural space for brief warmth; prompt tuning is a low-risk, fast test of whether the desired effect is achievable without weight changes.
outcome: A decision-ready document was written recommending prompt tuning as the first path, with rebuild contingent on tuning failing to achieve the desired register.

context: A model training study was evaluating whether to combine reasoning improvements from newer adapter versions (v0.3.4/v0.3.5) with guardrails from an earlier stable version (v0.3.2) before running any GPU experiments.
options: Focus only on the latest version's capabilities
Prepare a study combining reasoning from newer versions with guardrails from the stable earlier version
Defer the study until more evaluation data is available
choice: Prepare a study and experiment plan that combines reasoning from the newer adapters with guardrails from the earlier stable version, creating a decision-ready document before any runs are executed.
rationale: The newer versions improved reasoning but the earlier stable version had established guardrails. Combining them could leverage strengths of both. Running any GPU experiment first would waste compute if the combination proved structurally incompatible. A study document gates compute spend on architectural clarity.
outcome: A study document was created and marked decision-ready, with no GPU runs executed until the operator reviewed the plan.

context: A model training study had concluded that the reasoning-framework 7B model had reached a performance ceiling. The team was reviewing whether that conclusion was warranted given the search space actually covered.
options: Accept the ceiling conclusion and move on to other improvements
Push back: argue the search was incomplete and a key experiment had not been run
choice: Push back on the ceiling assumption, concluding the search was incomplete and the key merge experiment had not been attempted.
rationale: Three grounded points: (1) only three hyperparameter knobs had been varied; (2) the merge lever was entirely unexplored despite being structurally ideal for sibling adapters sharing a parent model; (3) sibling adapters sharing a parent make weight-space merging particularly promising. The prior ceiling conclusion was premature given these untried levers.
outcome: The study was updated to plan the cheapest experiment that could settle the question — a sibling-adapter merge — rather than accepting the ceiling.

context: Two parallel experiments were running: a heavy GPU merge sweep on a cloud pod and a lightweight premise-check scaffold on a local compute node. Both would produce large outputs if polled naively.
options: Pull back full pod and eval outputs for detailed in-context analysis
Pull back only compact scored verdicts to keep the working context clean
choice: Pull back only compact scored verdicts from both experiments.
rationale: Full outputs from a GPU merge sweep would clutter the working context significantly and make it harder to manage parallel workstreams. Compact scored verdicts provide sufficient signal to evaluate experiment success and make routing decisions without blowing up context size.
outcome: Both experiments ran in the background; only compact scored summaries were surfaced at completion, keeping the coordinating session context manageable.

context: A background agent was watching a GPU pod mid-run, but the SendMessage tool was unavailable in the current session context, making it impossible to pass the watch over to a new agent without first establishing pod state.
options: Attempt to use SendMessage despite unavailability
Perform a cheap pod-status check first, then hand over to a fresh background agent with the pod ID already known
choice: Confirm the pod was running and retrieve its ID cheaply, then hand over to a fresh background agent armed with that information.
rationale: Without SendMessage, the hand-over required a different path. Confirming pod status and ID before spawning the takeover agent avoided redundant rediscovery work by the new agent and allowed setting an accurate billing backstop. The cheap check cost negligible time vs. the risk of the takeover agent failing to find the pod.
outcome: The fresh background agent took over the watch with the pod ID pre-confirmed, avoiding rediscovery overhead and enabling an accurate billing ceiling.

context: A premise-check scaffold was evaluated against the baseline reasoning-framework model (v0.3.2) on held-out test items. The scaffold improved held-out test pass rate from 6/10 to 9/10 but degraded performance on some items already handled well by the current version.
options: Accept the scaffold despite degradation on some items because held-out gains outweigh losses
Fail the scaffold at the gate because it is net-negative on the original metric
Continue iterating on the scaffold before making a gate decision
choice: Fail the scaffold at the gate: net-negative on the original metric is disqualifying even with held-out gains.
rationale: The degradation affected capabilities the current version already handled reliably. A scaffold that regresses existing behavior while improving held-out traps does not pass the quality gate, because the in-distribution degradation is a direct cost to deployed users while the held-out gain is a theoretical future benefit. The gate criteria were defined upfront.
outcome: Task marked completed with failed-gate status, signaling that this scaffold iteration is not deployable as-is and the approach requires rethinking before the next attempt.

context: A GPU pod merge pipeline was running; prior runs had each hit different fixable blockers. The team was on run #3 and needed to decide how long to tolerate the process before intervening.
options: Let the process continue indefinitely without an intervention trigger
Set a concrete intervention trigger: step in if run #4 produces no candidate scores
choice: Monitor passively through run #3 but commit to stepping in at run #4 if no candidate scores are produced.
rationale: Three runs with fixable per-run blockers is reasonable convergence behavior for a pipeline being hardened. But tolerating a fourth failed run without scores would waste GPU spend for no information gain. Setting the trigger at run #4 gives a concrete resource fence while not over-reacting to the normal per-run debugging pattern.
outcome: The process completed run #3; the team held with the intervention trigger armed rather than preemptively stopping.

context: A GPU merge sweep completed and produced candidates that needed to be scored against a held-out reasoning dataset. The scorer's calibration level needed to be determined before use.
options: Apply the study's original scoring range strictly with no adjustment
Calibrate the scorer at or slightly above the study's grading and manually eyeball borderline candidates
choice: Calibrate the scorer at or slightly above the study grading and reserve manual review for candidates scoring exactly at the cutoff.
rationale: Strict application of the original range risks false negatives on candidates that are marginally below the threshold. Calibrating slightly above the study grading gives a consistent gate while manual eyeballing of borderline cases preserves a human check to catch misclassification at the boundary. The two-layer approach avoids both over-rejection and blind auto-pass.
outcome: The scorer was calibrated and ready for use; borderline candidates flagged for human review before final ranking.

context: A reasoning-framework model was about to switch to a newly announced frontier model that used an unexpected naming convention. The assistant's knowledge cutoff predated the announcement, and the model name did not match the provider's known naming patterns.
options: Switch the session model immediately as instructed
Refuse to switch until verifying the new model's authenticity via web search
Ignore the prompt and continue on the current model
choice: Refuse to switch the session model until the new model's existence could be verified via web search.
rationale: The announced model name did not match the provider's established naming conventions. Acting on an unverified announcement risks routing work to a non-existent or mislabeled model. The cost of a quick web verification was negligible; the cost of a mistaken switch mid-session could invalidate work in progress.
outcome: A web search was run to verify the model before any session switch occurred.

context: A new frontier model had just been confirmed available. A portfolio read had previously been conducted using an older model version, and the team wanted to conduct a fresh read with the new model for comparison.
options: Use fresh invocation flags and framing for the new model read
Reuse the exact invocation flags and framing from the prior model version read for comparability
Create a new template from scratch
choice: Reuse the exact invocation flags and framing from the prior read to enable an apples-to-apples comparison.
rationale: The value of the new read was primarily the delta between model versions, not novelty of framing. Changing flags or framing simultaneously would conflate model differences with methodology differences, making the comparison uninformative. Reusing the prior template isolates the model as the only variable.
outcome: The new model read was framed identically to the prior version read, enabling direct comparison of model outputs on the same portfolio context.

context: A new frontier model became available mid-session while an ongoing analysis was underway using the previous model version. The current session had an accuracy-annotation companion that would be invalidated if the model changed mid-run.
options: Switch to the new model immediately within the current session
Finish the current session on the existing model and make the new model the default for new sessions
Delay adopting the new model until after further evaluation
choice: Finish the current session on the existing model; make the new model the default only for new sessions going forward.
rationale: The current session had an in-progress accuracy-annotation companion designed for the existing model. Switching mid-session would break the companion's coherence and invalidate the consistency of annotations collected so far. The new model was free until a known cutoff date, so adoption cost nothing by deferring one session.
outcome: The current session completed on the original model; the new model was designated default for subsequent sessions, and a parallel portfolio read was kicked off using the new model for comparison.

context: A GPU merge sweep had scored a perfect 10/10 on held-out reasoning tasks. A decision was needed on whether to finalize the merge verdict or wait for additional evaluation dimensions.
options: Accept the 10/10 reasoning score and finalize the merge verdict now
Hold the verdict until the per-candidate breakdown on the deciding axis and additional blend candidates are also analyzed
choice: Hold the merge verdict pending the per-candidate breakdown on the deciding axis and analysis of additional blend candidates.
rationale: A perfect score on held-out reasoning tasks is strong but not conclusive if the deciding axis (register/audit-arm quality) has not yet been evaluated. A merge that wins on reasoning but regresses on the critical audit-arm behavior would be a net loss. Waiting 3.5 hours of merge time for a complete picture is cheaper than deploying a regression and rolling back.
outcome: The merge sweep continued running to produce the full per-candidate breakdown before any verdict was recorded.

context: After reconciling multiple task ledgers, the team was preparing to commit and push changes to four private remote repositories. JSON edits to ledger files had been made by hand.
options: Commit and push immediately without validation
Validate the JSON edits first, then commit and push
Defer committing until manual review by the operator
choice: Validate all JSON edits before committing and pushing.
rationale: Hand-edited JSON is a common source of syntax errors. Committing corrupted ledger data to four repositories simultaneously would require a corrective commit across all four repos. The validation step is cheap (a single parse call per file) and blocks a multi-repo cleanup. Deferring to the operator added unnecessary delay.
outcome: JSON validation ran, edits were confirmed clean, and all four repositories received clean commits.

context: A GPU model-training run was executing on an independent cloud pod. The team was deciding whether it was safe to restart the coordinating session and switch to a new model version.
options: Delay restart until the GPU run finishes to avoid any risk
Restart immediately given the GPU run persists on its own pod regardless of the coordinating session
choice: Restart immediately: all repositories were committed and pushed, session notes were written, and the GPU run was confirmed to operate independently on its own pod.
rationale: The GPU run's persistence was not tied to the coordinating session — it runs on a self-contained cloud pod with its own termination logic. With clean repos and written session notes providing a resumption point, there was no work at risk. Waiting would delay the model switch with no benefit.
outcome: The session was closed and restarted on the new model version; the GPU run continued autonomously and completed, sending results to the model hub.

context: A new pre-commit source validation check was added to a training pipeline. Before relying on it in production, the team needed to verify the check worked correctly.
options: Trust the new source check without additional testing
Run only a syntax check
Run a full triple-gate: syntax check, dry-run on valid input, and negative test confirming the check rejects the old broken format
choice: Run all three verification gates: syntax check, dry-run on valid input, and negative test with the old broken format.
rationale: A source check that passes syntax but fails to actually catch the broken format it was built to block is worse than useless — it provides false confidence. The negative test is the load-bearing gate because it confirms the check catches the specific failure mode it was designed for, not just that it runs without errors.
outcome: All three gates passed; the new source check was confirmed to reject the old broken format while accepting valid input.

context: A training environment file (the small model.env) failed a bash -u source check, indicating broken or missing variable bindings. The training runner was deciding whether to proceed with the broken environment or halt.
options: Proceed with the broken environment and handle failures downstream
Reject the broken environment immediately and do not fire the training run
Attempt to auto-repair the environment before firing
choice: Reject the broken environment and halt without firing.
rationale: Proceeding with a broken environment would produce unpredictable training failures at arbitrary points downstream, making debugging harder and potentially corrupting intermediate state. Auto-repair without knowing the cause of the failure is risky. The bash -u source check is an intentional pre-flight gate; honoring it is the point. Halt, reset the working directory, surface the issue.
outcome: The environment was rejected, the shell working directory was reset, and the runner did not fire — leaving the issue for diagnosis rather than masking it.

context: A cloud GPU run for a model training sweep failed with a shell-quoting bug: unquoted base model names containing pipe characters caused bash to misinterpret the config line as a pipeline. The watchdog caught the failure and killed the pod quickly, but two prior runs had already failed to fixable blockers.
options: Fix the quoting in the config and add a dry-run pre-flight that only prints the config without testing it
Fix the quoting and add a pre-flight gate that actually test-loads the config locally before any pod is created
Leave the config as-is and accept repeated failures
choice: Fix the quoting issue and add a pre-flight gate that test-loads the config locally before any pod creation attempt.
rationale: A dry-run that only prints the config would not have caught the quoting bug because the bug was a bash parsing failure at execution, not a content error. Test-loading the config in the local environment (where no pod billing occurs) catches parse-time failures at zero cost. The pod billing only starts on creation, so catching the error before creation is strictly better than catching it after.
outcome: The quoting bug was fixed; the pre-flight gate was verified to reject a bad config; the pod run was re-fired and completed without failure.

context: Two scheduled automation tasks were both configured to fire at 07:30, creating a race condition between them.
options: Leave both tasks at 07:30 and handle the race condition in code
Move the lower-priority fallback task to 08:00 in both the canonical config and the installed launchd plists, then reload
Disable one of the tasks to eliminate the conflict
choice: Move the fallback task to 08:00 in both the canonical config and the installed plists, then reload both.
rationale: Disabling a task removes functionality. Handling the race in code adds complexity and still risks edge-case collisions. Simply separating the fire times by 30 minutes eliminates the race cleanly. Updating both the canonical config and the installed plist ensures the change persists across reinstalls and is not just a live-system patch.
outcome: The timing conflict was resolved; both tasks fire at different times and the plist reload confirmed the change took effect.

context: A roguelike RPG was undergoing a focused polish pass. A tarot card omen system was proposed as an addition during the polish phase, but it introduced entirely new game mechanics rather than refining existing ones.
options: Include omen-card system work in the current polish plan
Park the omen-card system as out-of-scope for the current polish pass because it constitutes a new mechanic
choice: Park the omen-card system: it is a new mechanic, not polish, and does not belong in the scoped polish pass.
rationale: Scope creep during a polish pass risks destabilizing tested systems and extending the delivery timeline indefinitely. Keeping polish strictly limited to polish — refinement of existing behavior — and deferring new mechanics to a separate design pass maintains the coherence of the current work and produces a cleaner deliverable.
outcome: The omen-card system was explicitly deferred in the commit message, keeping the polish plan scoped to polish only.

context: A roguelike RPG already had bespoke period-correct card art for its Karnoffel card game sub-system. A newly licensed tarot card asset set was available and needed placement. The game's themes include Reformation-era supernatural beliefs and heresy.
options: Use tarot cards in the existing Karnoffel card game sub-system, replacing or supplementing the current art
Place tarot cards in the supernatural-folklore event thread as omen or event imagery
Do not use the tarot cards in the game
choice: Keep the Karnoffel system's existing period-correct art unchanged; route the tarot cards to the supernatural-folklore event thread as thematic omen imagery.
rationale: Karnoffel already has German-suited card art appropriate to its 1527 setting. Replacing it with tarot would be historically redundant and less authentic. The tarot cards as divination instruments are anachronistic to the setting, but that anachronism is a thematic feature — it reflects superstition and folk belief in a game about heresy and religious conflict. Placing them in the supernatural-folklore thread turns the anachronism into a design asset rather than a problem.
outcome: Tarot cards scoped out of Karnoffel and into the supernatural-folklore mechanic thread, pending final feel-call from the designer.

context: A roguelike RPG's tarot card reader feature was being implemented. Design choices included how to gate the encounters and how resolved major arcana cards affect the game state.
options: Implement a simple card reader with flip animation only
Implement with night-gated encounters, major arcana resolving into omen groups that affect gameplay outcomes and faction reputation
choice: Implement the card reader with night-gated encounters, omen groups, and faction reputation effects tied to existing Supernatural Evidence and faction systems.
rationale: Night-gating ties the mechanic to existing rest/night-cycle systems, making it coherent rather than free-floating. Omen groups connected to faction reputation integrate the feature into the existing reputation economy rather than creating an isolated side mechanic. Tying into existing systems reduces implementation surface while increasing narrative depth.
outcome: Card reader feature committed and pushed, ready for dogfood testing with the described mechanic integrations active.

context: Agreement evaluation was run on three the small model model candidates (gemma-270m q4_k_m, smollm2-360m q4_k_m, qwen25-05b q4_k_m) against 150 holdout plans using quadrant-agreement and format-compliance quality gates.
options: Select the best performer (qwen25-05b) despite failing the quality gates
Reject all three models because none pass the gates
choice: Reject all three models: none pass the quadrant-agreement or format-compliance gates. qwen25-05b noted as the best performer with the highest quadrant discrimination, but still failing.
rationale: Deploying a model that fails defined quality gates because it is the least-bad option lowers the bar for future gate-failures and sends a false signal that gate failures are acceptable. The gates exist to define a minimum viable quality floor; if none of the candidates clear it, the right response is to improve the training approach, not lower the bar.
outcome: Evaluation artifacts committed with explicit gate-failure documentation; no model promoted to deployment.

context: Three the small model model candidates all failed quality gates (best result: 38%/61% on quadrant agreement vs. the required threshold). A v002 training plan was being designed to address the failures.
options: Continue iterating on v001 with minor changes

Design a v002 plan: scale the plan bank to ~2000 examples, use a stronger teacher model for generation, run overnight 7B teaching on the local bot, and enforce a four-slot grammar via llama.cpp GBNF at inference time
Change the model architecture or abandon the project
choice: Design v002 with four targeted changes: scale plan bank to ~2000 examples, DeepSeek-based generation, overnight 7B teaching pass, and GBNF four-slot grammar enforcement at inference.
rationale: v001's failures were diagnosable: insufficient training data volume (plan bank too small), weak teacher quality (not using the best available teacher model), no structural grammar enforcement at inference causing format failures. Each v002 change targets a specific diagnosed failure mode. Changing architecture or abandoning would discard training infrastructure that is otherwise sound.
outcome: v002 task created and queued for operator approval; v001 declared terminal.

context: A reasoning-framework 7B model's production Telegram chat path had been upgraded to support sequential decision sessions — multi-step decision chains with slot-locking. The upgrade reused the existing bot-side engine and proxy plumbing.
options: Develop a new engine for sequential decision sessions
Reuse the existing bot-side engine and proxy plumbing, which had been smoke-verified earlier that day
choice: Reuse the existing engine and proxy plumbing for sequential decision sessions.
rationale: The existing engine had been smoke-verified in production that same day. Building a new engine would duplicate infrastructure and introduce new failure modes. The existing plumbing already handled the authentication and dispatch path that sequential sessions needed; the only addition was the slot-locking orchestration layer above it.
outcome: Sequential decision sessions were live on the Telegram path; the harness assembled final answers from multi-step chains in chat. Nearly all pending tasks closed as a result.

context: A the small model model v002 candidate was being designed. v001 had failed quality gates partly because of structural output breakdown — the model produced broken fragments. A grammar constraint (GBNF four-slot schema) was proposed to enforce output structure at inference time. The question was whether grammar enforcement would dilute the model's trained character register.
options: Use v001 without grammar enforcement, accepting structural breakdown risk
Use v002 with GBNF grammar enforcement, trading off some expressive freedom for structural reliability
Abandon grammar constraints to allow fully free-form output
choice: Use GBNF grammar enforcement in v002: it enforces only the structural skeleton while leaving internal word choice and style free.
rationale: v001's broken fragments were a structural failure, not a voice failure. The grammar schema enforces only the slot boundaries (not the words within them), so the model's trained voice and judgment style remain fully model-generated inside the constrained slots. The constraint prevents collapse of the output shape without touching the character of the content.
outcome: v002 design locked to include GBNF four-slot grammar enforcement at inference; documented rationale that grammar protects, not dilutes, the model's character.

context: A corpus-ingestion workflow resumed after hitting an API rate limit mid-run. The resumed workflow had inherited the original model configuration. Mid-resume, the team considered switching to a different (cheaper) model for the remaining items.
options: Flip the model on the resumed workflow and continue resuming
Stop the resume, preserve all completed results on disk, and run a fresh workflow with the new model routing over only the missing items
choice: Stop the resume, keep all completed results, and run a fresh workflow over missing items only.
rationale: The resume cache was keyed on agent options including model. Changing the model mid-resume would invalidate the cache and force re-running 17 already-completed items under the new model — the opposite of cost-saving. Stopping the resume, preserving completed results, and running fresh over missing items achieves the model-switch goal without redundant recomputation.
outcome: Resumed workflow halted; existing results preserved; fresh Sonnet-routed workflow run over missing items only, with no redundant re-runs.

context: A nightly autonomous training pipeline was being designed for a local model training run. The pipeline included corpus building, sweep firing, model selection, and deployment. Agreement evaluation was the final quality gate step.
options: Automate the entire pipeline end-to-end including agreement evaluation
Automate all steps except agreement evaluation, which stays as a manual operator step
choice: Automate all steps except agreement evaluation, which remains a manual step run by the operator when available.
rationale: Agreement evaluation is the go/no-go gate for promoting a new model version to production. An incorrect auto-promotion could degrade a deployed model serving live users. All other steps (corpus building, sweep execution, model archiving) are mechanically verifiable and safe to automate. Keeping the human in the loop at the final gate preserves quality control on the highest-consequence decision in the pipeline.
outcome: Pipeline runs autonomously on the compute node; the operator spends approximately 10 minutes on agreement evaluation when available, then makes the promotion decision manually.

context: A roguelike RPG's tarot card reader scene displayed printed card titles from a licensed art asset that did not match the character's spoken dialog lines, creating a visible mismatch. Options for resolving the mismatch were considered.
options: Crop the title band to remove the credit line and eliminate the mismatch
Add a caption overlay to explain the mismatch in the UI
Leave the art as-is with the mismatch
Recompose the cards to better match the spoken lines
choice: Leave the art entirely as-is with the mismatch; mark the issue resolved.
rationale: Cropping the title band would modify the credited artist's work (the asset license requires unmodified use). Recomposed cards would look worse than the current mismatch. A caption overlay adds UI elements to a deliberately clean reveal scene. The mismatch is defensible as period texture — the foreign printed card titles versus the character's spoken lines can read as authentic flavor reflecting the strangeness of the printed artifact. A revisit path was documented: if dogfooding proves the mismatch reads as a bug rather than flavor, a small caption can be added with minimal effort.
outcome: Art left unchanged; issue closed; revisit path documented in the commit message for dogfood follow-up.
